agency_open_data_plan:
The Bureau of Labor and Industries (BOLI) protects employment rights, advances employment opportunities, and protects access to housing and public accommodations free from discrimination for all Oregonians. It also regulates and supports apprenticeship programs to develop Oregonâ€™s workforce. 

The agency has five main areas that collect and maintain data: Commissionerâ€™s Office, Civil Rights, Wage and Hour, Apprenticeship, and Employer Assistance. The agency operates out of three physical office locations and has remote employees providing services throughout the state. 

BOLI is currently in the planning phase of the Open Data effort. The agency intends to develop a Data Inventory Plan by March 1, 2023, and complete a Data Inventory by June 1, 2023. As the agency prepares to embark on a database replacement project over the next few years, opportunities for open data will be identified.

agency_name: Office of the State Fire Marshal
agency_open_data_plan:
"The Oregon Office of State Fire Marshalâ€™s (OSFM) mission is to protect people, property, and the environment from fire and hazardous materials. The office carries out these duties through prevention education, inspections, code enforcement, and preparedness and response activities. The Office currently resides in the Department of State Police but will become its own agency effective July 1, 2023. 
OSFM experiences a significant workload increase between June and October each year as we make resources available for wildfire response. We are also in the middle of a two-year transition to becoming an independent agency. 
We have requested and been granted an extension of the deadline to submit our inventory until March 1, 2023. We will begin our initial inventory and data identification on June 3, 2022 with a meeting of our Executive Leadership Team.
"

The Oregon State Marine Board (OSMB) is Oregon's recreational boating agency. Our mission is to serve Oregon's recreational boating public through education, enforcement, access, and environmental stewardship for a safe and enjoyable experience.
For the current biennium 2021-2023, OSMB is in the planning phase to meet the Open Data Standard requirements. Our goal is to bring a selection of high-priority datasets to a five-star open data rating. The datasets identified in OSMB's inventory embody the data created by the agency for each section and program area to carry out its mission.
OSMB has completed an initial inventory and identified various datasets and reports accessible through ArcGIS Online in Maps, Applications, and Dashboards, at oregon.gov/osmb/, or on data.oregon.gov as the readiest datasets and reports to publish this biennium. A few of the publicly available datasets most ready are GIS datasets that include boating access sites, boated waterways, regulations and rules, clean vessel facilities, and boating obstructions. Preparation and publication of the datasets and reports will continue through the biennium while the Agency works on its Data Governance Plan.

The Oregon Board of Pharmacy serves to promote and protect public health, safety, and welfare by ensuring high standards in the practice of pharmacy and through effective regulation of the manufacture and distribution of drugs. 

Our Vision â€“ Partners for a Healthy Oregon

Our Values - These values reflect both how our Board and staff strive to conduct ourselves, and the behaviors we seek to instill across the practice of pharmacy in Oregon.

â€¢	Quality - We strive to deliver a consistent standard of excellence *Excellence *Value *Worth  
â€¢	Safety - We are committed to protecting the health, safety and welfare of the public *Protection *Security 
        *Care
â€¢	Integrity - We meet commitments to public health & safety and are accountable for our words and actions 
        *Honesty *Ethics *Respect
â€¢	Professionalism - We are committed to promoting excellence in pharmacy practice *Expertise *Commitment 
        *Competence
â€¢	Accountability - We accept responsibility for our actions, products, decisions and policies *Trust 
        *Responsibility *Transparency 

The agency is currently in the planning phase and working through the process of identifying data system inventories. The agency estimates that it will complete the data system inventory in July 2022. The Oregon Board of Pharmacy supports the Oregon Open Data Program and has prioritized completing the data system inventory process and identifying possible datasets for future publication.

agency_name: Board of Examiners for Speech-Language Pathology & Audiology

agency_open_data_plan:
The mission of Construction Contractors Board (CCB) is to protect and Serve Oregon Consumers, support responsible licensed contractors and promote a positive business climate.  We do that by delivering customer-focused, outcome-based programs that support a fair and competitive construction industry and robust consumer protections. The datasets captured within the CCB inventory each support one or more of the functions necessary to accomplish our mission.

CCB will focus on publication of the â€œLicense Information â€“ Currently Activeâ€ dataset during the upcoming biennium.  Because CCB is currently working on replacing the core business software for all our functions, work on this effort will begin after the start of the fiscal year and will encompass that single dataset on data.oregon.gov.

The Criminal Justice Commission (CJC) works to improve the legitimacy, efficiency, and effectiveness of state and local criminal justice systems. The purpose of the Commission is to improve the effectiveness and efficiency of state and local criminal justice systems by providing a centralized and impartial forum for statewide policy development and planning. The Oregon Statistical Analysis Center (SAC) is housed at CJC and is the stateâ€™s clearinghouse for criminal justice data and research. As the stateâ€™s Statistical Analysis Center, the agency provides the Governor, Legislature, and public safety partners with access to data, objective research, and analysis related to public safety.
While CJC has access to many criminal justice data sources, the agency primarily collects a more limited number of data sources. CJC has inventoried six datasets and plans to focus publication on the STOP (Statistical Transparency of Policing) data as the agencyâ€™s most valuable data source. CJC will work to publish this data on data.oregon.gov by the end of the current biennium.

agency_name: Department of Consumer and Business Services
agency_open_data_plan:
The Oregon Department of Consumer and Business Services (DCBS) works to protect and serve Oregonâ€™s consumers and workers while supporting a positive business climate. As Oregonâ€™s largest consumer protection and business regulatory agency, DCBS is a resource to consumers and businesses in areas involving: finance (insurance, investments, Oregon-chartered banks and credit unions, consumer finance companies, mortgage lenders, pawnbrokers, and payday lenders), workplace health and safety, and building safety. 

DCBS has finished creating its initial agency data inventory of 96 datasets and identified data related to Oregon OSHA's enforcement activities, including inspections, citations, violations, and penalties, as the most valuable resources to focus on publishing at this time. We will enter a planning phase to prioritize and prepare these types of datasets for publication to the Oregon Open Data Portal (data.oregon.gov) during the 2023-25 biennium. We will also evaluate currently published datasets and analyze the need for modifications to ensure they meet the standards set by the Open Data Program. The agency anticipates being able to pilot automated publishing and updating of these datasets to data.oregon.gov during the 2023-25 biennium.

The Department of Environmental Quality (DEQ)â€™s mission is to be a leader in restoring, maintaining and enhancing the quality of Oregon's air, land and water. From monitoring and assessing the quality of our air, water and soil, through regulation that reduces pollution, and looking ahead to what climate change may bring, DEQ supports businesses, communities, and municipalities statewide to preserve and protect Oregon's natural resources and human health.

The datasets captured within the DEQ inventory encapsulate the data created, received, or maintained as each program area carries out their daily work. DEQ submitted a partial inventory of 22 datasets and plans to submit the remaining inventory of approximately 80-100 by August, 2022. DEQ will remain in the planning stage this biennium as we complete the data inventory and identify priority datasets for future publication. During this planning phase we do plan to publish the DEQ public records requests dataset as a pilot project.

The Oregon Department of Human Services (ODHS) is Oregonâ€™s principal agency for helping Oregonians achieve wellbeing and independence through opportunities that protect, empower, respect choice, and preserve dignity, especially for those who are least able to help themselves. Achieving equity is foundational to our work, and we are committed to integrating equity into all that we do. ODHS provides direct service to more than 1 million Oregonians each year. These services provide a key safety net for those in our society who are most vulnerable or who are at a difficult place in their life. Key functions serve children, adults, families, seniors, and people with disabilities through programs such as food stamps, Temporary Assistance for Needy Families, vocational rehabilitation, and many others.

Together, ODHS and the Oregon Health Authority (OHA) provide critical services to Oregonians services including health care, food assistance, programs for people with disabilities and many others. Due to the complex and interrelated nature our work, OHA and ODHS are partnering to provide one joint inventory of agency datasets and are coordinating on efforts to inventory, model, and map data while planning for enterprise data management and data governance. 

ODHS and OHA are in the PLANNING phase of the Open Data project. We do not anticipate publishing any datasets during the current biennium (beyond those that are already published) due to the preparation and resourcing required to identify and prepare data for publication. The agency is only now beginning its inventory, mapping, modeling, management, and governance planning. Due to the quantity of data systems, number of distinct program areas, and number of data collections, much work remains to identify all datasets and answer Open Data project questions related to each.

agency_name: Department of Land Conservation and Development
agency_open_data_plan: The Department of Land Conservation and Development (DLCD) is in a planning phase for the Open Data Project. The majority of DLCD datasets are already publicly available via our Oregon.gov website or other sources. Due to staff resource constraints, we have had limited capacity to devote the necessary time to develop the plan for preparing the other datasets to be published. We anticipate this task to be taken on by a new agency CIO once that position is successfully filled early 2023.

The Oregon Department of Corrections (ODOC) is responsible for managing the corrections system in the state of Oregon. ODOC's primary mission is to promote public safety and reduce recidivism by supervising and providing rehabilitative services to individuals in custody, on parole, or on probation. ODOC oversees a network of correctional facilities and programs aimed at addressing the needs of Adults in Custody (AIC), helping them transition back into the community, and ensuring the secure confinement of those sentenced to incarceration. 
 
The ODOC is dedicated to the principles of transparency, accountability, and public engagement. Our open data initiative is guided by the following objectives:
â€¢	Enhancing Transparency: ODOC aims to provide the public with accessible, accurate, and up-to-date information about our operations and corrections system.
â€¢	Fostering Accountability: We believe that accountability is fostered through data transparency. By making data available, we enable the public to assess our performance and hold us accountable.
â€¢	Promoting Innovation: ODOC recognizes that valuable insights and applications can be derived from our data. We encourage innovation by allowing developers, researchers, and the public to leverage our data to generate solutions and improvements.
â€¢	Protecting Privacy: We are committed to safeguarding the personal and sensitive information of individuals involved in the corrections system. Data release will be governed by strict privacy measures.
â€¢	Legal and Ethical Compliance: ODOC will ensure that data release aligns with all applicable federal, state, and local laws, as well as ethical guidelines.
The ODOCâ€™s Open Data Plan aims to address the unique challenge of managing and sharing data that is entered into outdated and disparate systems. Our primary Correctional Information Systems contain information for both AICs within a facility and in the community that include a wide set of data regarding demographics, sentencing, offense, classification, assessments, screenings, behavioral performance, case plans, medication information, participating in rehabilitation and programming, release planning, employment, visitation, grievance, incident reports, use of force, audits, policies, and other operations information. 
 
While disparate data systems can present complexities, ODOC remains determined to harness the full potential of our data assets for the benefit of our stakeholders, and the public, while upholding privacy and security standards. ODOC will continue actively working towards establishing an effective open data inventory and is committed to cataloging all relevant data sets, creating a user-friendly data catalog with comprehensive metadata, and prioritizing the release of data that enhances public understanding. 
 
To achieve this, ODOC will establish robust data governance policies to ensure data quality and privacy compliance, engaging with stakeholders for their input, and developing user-friendly access tools, such as APIs and data visualizations. By consistently updating the inventory and fostering transparency through regular reports, the department is focused on enhancing accountability and community engagement, ultimately contributing to a more informed and open criminal justice system.

agency_name: Department of Geology And Mineral Industries
agency_open_data_plan:
DOGAMI helps increase understanding of Oregonâ€™s geologic resources and hazards through science and stewardship. The Geological Survey & Services (GSS) program develops maps, reports, and data to help Oregon manage natural resources and prepare for natural hazards with focus areas including geologic hazards (earthquakes, landslides, floods, and coastal erosion), geologic mapping, and high-resolution lidar data collection. The Mineral Land Regulation & Reclamation program oversees the stateâ€™s mineral production and works to minimize impacts of natural resource extraction and maximize opportunities for land reclamation. 

DOGAMI has undertaken the following activities in support of completing a data inventory:
â€¢	Convened a joint meeting of representatives from both agency programs (Mineral Land Regulation & Reclamation and Geologic Survey & Services) to determine the scope of data to be included in the inventory;
â€¢	Attended a detailed and informative meeting with the Data Governance and Transparency office;
â€¢	Identified program areas where data is to be gathered; and
â€¢	Identified the locations of DOGAMI publication data inventories.

While efforts to find qualifying data continue, DOGAMI has so far identified one dataset for publishing on data.oregon.gov. DOGAMIâ€™s GSS program publishes geoscience research papers, bulletins, fact sheets, maps, and other documents to disseminate DOGAMIâ€™s research findings to the public. The publications are currently housed in a searchable repository on the DOGAMI website that contains publications dated as early as 1937. New publications are added to the dataset frequently.

Through these activities, DOGAMI has determined that with the large quantity and diversity of data, and the limited capacity of staff, that a more realistic and achievable deadline for completion and submission of the Open Data inventory is December 1, 2022.

The Oregon Department of Justice aims to be open, transparent, and accountable to our stakeholders
and foster constructive dialogue and informed decision making. We are committed to leveraging open
data and technology standards to further our mission. We plan to make data available, where possible,
to the public to help monitor, evaluate, and improve services, support collaboration, and allow insights
into publicly available activities.

Of paramount is the need to protect public safety, privacy, and confidentiality when releasing data. The
development of our open data plan will ensure data is released responsibility. We will selectively
publish datasets to meet our stakeholderâ€™s needs, while ensuring the appropriate technical and legal
controls remain in place. This includes properly verifying data, releasing data through appropriate
channels, and ensuring the data follows privacy and security best practices. Our open data plan will
include providing resources to our stakeholders such as open data principles, data quality, and
standards.

Oregon Department of Revenue Open Data Plan 
Approved by Revenue Leadership Team May 19, 2022
for June 2022 through June 2024

The Oregon Department of Revenue administers Oregon tax laws, supports partnersâ€™ programs, and acts as a central debt collection agency for other state agencies, boards, commissions, and local governments. We provide services for the general public, tax professionals, cities, counties, local taxing districts, tribal governments, and other state agencies.  The datasets captured within the DOR inventory have to do with tax returns, operations, and financial information for 57 revenue streams managed by the Department.  The nature of tax data includes highly confidential information, which cannot be made public in its original form.
DOR has inventoried 187 datasets, approximately 39 of which are already published in some form by DOR or another agency.  For this cycle, the Department intends to allocate the resources and design the processes required to sustain the transferring of datasets already made available online to the data.oregon.gov website and keeping them up to date. As part of this effort, DOR will pilot posting one or more existing datasetsâ€”which it has already published onlineâ€”to the data.oregon.gov website.  Further planning work will entail prioritizing potential datasets to be published in the 24-26 open data cycle.

agency_name: Department of Public Safety Standards and Training

agency_open_data_plan:
Upon statehood, the Federal government granted Oregon 3.4 million acres of school lands to support public education. About 775,000 acres of school lands remain in state ownership today, the revenue from which helps fund K-12 public education. The Oregon Department of State Lands manages school lands and oversees the stateâ€™s publicly-owned waterways on behalf of the State Land Board. Over time, the Legislature has expanded the Departmentâ€™s responsibilities to also include protections of regulated waters and wetlands and oversight of Oregonâ€™s national estuarine research reserve. The Departmentâ€™s mission is to ensure Oregonâ€™s school land legacy and protect waterways and wetlands of the State through superior stewardship and service. The datasets we capture allow us to fulfill that mission.

DSL has inventoried 19 datasets, most of which are geographic information system (GIS) data that are already published to our own public-facing GIS Portal in the form of maps and data services. Of the remaining datasets, the Land Administration System (LAS) dataset has been identified as having the most value to publish this biennium. This system, which was created in 1999, is being replaced in the next year or so by the Oregon Waters and Land System (OWLS).  The underlying data structure will likely change, but it is still the most requested dataset we have. The permits we issue often have information that is of interest to local communities. People need to be able to find information about such projects easily. The LAS dataset will be migrated to OWLS and will be re-published with minimal anticipated impacts to data.oregon.gov. Publication of these datasets in their current form will start at the beginning of the fiscal year. We anticipate that between one and five datasets will be published on data.oregon.gov. As this data changes regularly, we hope to automate the process so it can be updated daily or weekly. Visualizations and project dashboards will also be created if time allows.

The Employment Relations Board (ERB) provides dispute resolution and administers the collective bargaining law that covers public employees of the State of Oregon and its cities, counties, school districts, and other local governments; hears and decides appeals from state employees concerning personnel actions; and administers the collective bargaining law that regulates private employers who are not covered by the National Labor Relations Act.
The ERB is comprised of the Board, the Hearings Office, and the State Conciliation Service. The three-member Board is a neutral quasi-judicial body whose members are appointed by the Governor and confirmed by the Senate. The Governor selects one member as Board Chair to serve as the agency administrator. Board members apply the law in a neutral and objective manner without regard to their background. 
The Hearings Office is the point-of-entry for contested case and representation filings.  This division conducts hearings on contested cases and issues recommended orders. 
The State Conciliation Service mediates labor relations disputes and provides labor-management and bargaining training to employers and employees. The State Conciliator is the head of the State Conciliation Service.   
The ERB data inventory includes the 10 datasets that will be published in the 23-25 biennium.  ERB will  annually publish the 10 existing datasets on data.oregon.gov.  ERB completes its agency data inventory in July of each fiscal year. ERB will continue to evaluate current published data to ensure this data meets the standards for open datasets set by the Open Data Program and revise or expand these datasets as necessary.

"The Higher Education Coordinating Commission (HECC) is the State of Oregonâ€™s Commission and agency working to improve equitable access to and success in higher education and workforce training for Oregonians statewide. The HECC administers about $1.8 billion per year in postsecondary education and workforce funding supporting community colleges, public universities, students, workforce boards, and other partners. We also coordinate postsecondary degrees, programs, and academic pathways; administer key Oregon financial aid, workforce, and other programs; evaluate the success of higher education efforts to guide progress; and provide strategic guidance to leaders and partners on a vision for Oregon higher education planning, funding, and policy. We directly serve Oregonians by awarding grant and scholarship aid to students, and connecting Oregonians with workforce and training resources.

HECC has inventoried 14 datasets and identified four related to workforce training programs and community colleges  as the ones for initial publication. These datasets focus on: 

1.)	Dislocated worker events
2.)	Workforce training programs receiving funding from the federal Workforce Innovation Opportunity Act (WIOA)
3.)	Courses of study offered by Oregon community colleges
4.)	Financial reports of the Oregon community colleges

Publication of these datasets will begin  in July of 2022.
"

The Office of the Long-Term Care Ombudsman (OLTCO) is an agency that functions separately and independently from any other state agency. The mission of the agency is to protect individual rights, promote independence, and ensure quality of life for Oregonians living in long-term care and residential facilities and for Oregonians with decisional limitations. 

The datasets identified in OLTCOâ€™s inventory embody the data created by each program as they carry out their functions and duties. OLTCO has completed an initial inventory and identified data within the RTZ Get Care dataset as the most valuable to publish this biennium. This dataset contains the complaint investigation and facility visitation from the Long-Term Care Ombudsman program.

Since the agency already reports federally (federal fiscal year â€“ Oct 1-Sept 30) on much of this information, the plan is to keep the same timeframes as is required for the agencyâ€™s federal reporting. 

The plan is to report complaint information: complaint codes (types of complaints received), status of those complaints â€“ closed and pending, facility type, city, county, status of complaint, referrals made to other entities, and possibly complainant type (resident, staff, family, etc.).

We will report data as of Oct. 2022 for this plan.

The Oregon Board of Dentistry (OBD) is charged with the regulation of the practice of dentistry, dental therapy and dental hygiene by setting standards for entry to practice, examination of applicants, issuance and renewal of licenses, and enforcing the standards of practice. The Board also is required by law to establish standards for the administration of anesthesia in dental offices. The Board determines dental procedures that may be delegated to dental assistants and establishes standards for training and certification of dental assistants. The mission of the Oregon Board of Dentistry is to promote quality oral healthcare and protect all communities in the State of Oregon by equitably and ethically regulating dental professionals.

The OBD has completed an initial inventory and identified data related to licensing and enforcement as the most ready and valued dataset to publish this biennium. This dataset covers OBDâ€™ enforcement activities and licensing data. Publication of this dataset will begin at the beginning of the fiscal year and should encompass 2-4 datasets on data.oregon.gov.

The Oregon Business Development Department, dba Business Oregon, is the stateâ€™s economic development agency. Business Oregon invests in Oregonâ€™s businesses, communities, and people to promote a globally competitive, diverse, and inclusive economy, in service of a vision of prosperity for all Oregonians. Core functions include rural community development and infrastructure financing; business retention, expansion, and recruitment; export promotion and international trade; industry research and development and entrepreneurship; small business assistance; and support for arts and cultural organizations. The overall work of the department is guided by a nine-member commission with additional input and expertise provided by several other advisory bodies and boards. Business Oregonâ€™s datasets include the data created as each agency program conducts its activities and daily work; an initial draft inventory of agency datasets includes the 30 datasets presently published by the agency. 

Business Oregonâ€™s Open Data Plan consists of two components: data publication and inventory completion. Business Oregon will continue publishing the 30 existing datasets on data.oregon.gov at an annual frequency and focus on ensuring that metadata and data dictionaries are updated and/or completed for all published datasets.  Business Oregon will also complete its initial agency data inventory in January 2023 and will further identify and prioritize additional datasets for publication on data.oregon.gov during the â€™23-â€˜25 biennium. As a part of the inventory activities, Business Oregon will also evaluate current published datasets to ensure these datasets meet the standards for open datasets set by the Open Data Program and revise or expand these datasets as necessary. Business Oregon commits to publishing 3-5 additional datasets beyond the current 30 on data.oregon.gov by the beginning of the 2024 fiscal year.

The Oregon Board of Medical Imaging has the authority to issue licenses and permits to medical imaging technologists and radiation therapists. With a mission to promote, preserve and protect the public health, safety and welfare of Oregonians who are undergoing medical imaging studies and radiation therapy performed by agency licensees the Board is dedicated to providing excellent service and support to the public, stakeholders, license and permit holders.

OBMI has inventoried 12 datasets related Applicant information, complaints, and professional training programs as the most valuable resources to publish this biennium.  Publication of these datasets will begin at the beginning of the fiscal year.

The mission of the Oregon Board of Naturopathic Medicine is to protect the public by licensing and regulating naturopathic physicians. The Board will promote physician excellence and will foster communication within the profession and with the public.  Oregon was the first state in the nation to establish a â€œBoard of Naturopathic Examiners,â€ granting it the power to license and regulate the practice of naturopathic medicine. Licensure is required to practice naturopathic medicine in the State of Oregon. Currently, There are 1200 active licensees in the state.

Board Responsibilities

Develops policy and sets standards of practice
Reviews applications and issues licenses to qualified applicants
Investigates complaints and takes appropriate disciplinary action when violations are found to have occurred
Monitors and enforces Oregon Revised Statutes and Administrative Rules pertaining to the practice of naturopathic medicine in the State of Oregon

The Board is committed to maintaining inventory of information assets and managing data sets.  The Board will ensure both the Executive Director and Administrative Specialist will engage in best practices; remain knowledgeable about Board data, its origins, and is available to answer questions about Board data and/or route questions to the appropriate parties.  The Executive Director and Administrative Specialist will ensure transparency of information that is not protected from disclosure, by making appropriate data available for public consumption, in a manner that will not jeopardize the privacy or security of agency employees, clients or partners.

Open Data Planâ€”Oregon Board of Optometry 2022:

The Oregon Board of Optometry (OBO) is a semi-independent state agency that is responsible for the licensing and regulation of the optometry profession in Oregon. The board was established and operates under the authority of ORS 683.010 to 683.340 and 676.110 to 676.230. OAR Chapter 852. OBO is a state agency with a strong commitment to serving the public, consumers, and licensees of the State of Oregon. The primary mission of OBO is consumer protection. This is achieved by promoting the highest standards for licensure through four (4) major program areas: Education, Examinations, Licensing, and Enforcement.

The OBO protects the public by issuing licenses to those individuals who have demonstrated professional competence and continue to maintain their professional competence through continued education. OBO is committed to regulating optometry in the State of Oregon without unnecessary regulatory oversight of licensees and by providing fair and accessible entry into the profession that meet the standards and requirements. OBO is a 2 person agency and is able to complete a license application within 1-2 days of receiving all required documentation.

OBO has inventoried eleven (11) datasets and identified datasets related to licensed optometrists licensure status as the most ready and valued dataset to publish this biennium. This dataset lists those businesses with active licenses. This dataset includes contact information. This dataset is already published on the OBOâ€™s website, and is available for ordering through the website.

The Oregon Board of Physical Therapy (OBPT) licenses and regulates physical therapists (PT) and physical therapists assistants (PTA) throughout the state. In the interest of public protection, the Board assures that PTs and PTAs are properly educated, hold current and valid licenses, practice within their scope, and continue to receive ongoing training throughout their careers. 

OBPT has inventoried 5 datasets, of which 3 are already publicly available through the Boardâ€™s public-facing licensing portal. These datasets are searchable, but the Board has identified that publishing an existing dataset on disciplinary actions in a different format for ease of use is a high priority. That work will be completed by the end of 2022, and will be published on data.oregon.gov as well as the Boardâ€™s own website.

Oregon Commission for the Blind (OCB) provides a multitude of services for blind and visually impaired Oregonians. OCB empowers these Oregonians by providing training, vocational rehabilitation, and adaptive equipment so they can thrive in their community and their workplace. The datasets captured within the OCB data inventory encapsulate the data created on a daily basis as our personnel work on individually tailored cases for our clients.

OCB has inventoried 13 datasets and identified datasets related to metrics regarding the needs of blind and visually impaired Oregonians as the most valuable and releasable resource to publish, but it will take time to sanitize this dataset to ensure that private personal information is not released to the public. Publication of this dataset should occur by end of calendar year 2022.

The Oregon Department of Agriculture (ODA) works to ensure healthy natural resources, a sustainable environment, and a thriving economy for Oregonians now and in the future, through inspection and certification, regulation, and promotion of agriculture and food. Supported by more than 400 full and part-time knowledgeable and dedicated staff, ODA serves a varied array of industries as a diverse state agency with approximately 36 individual programs in the areas of food safety; animal health; natural resources; market access and certification; plant protection and conservation and administration and support services.

The datasets captured within the ODA Data Inventory encapsulate the data created as each of these individual programs carry out their daily work activities and responsibilities. 
 
During the current biennium, ODA will continue to publish datasets on data.oregon.gov, on behalf of its Fertilizer Program including product registration, stop sale, use and removal orders, sample results and tonnage.  ODA will also continue to publish GIS/Map boundary and layer information on ArcGIS Online, regarding its Confined Animal Feeding Operation (CAFO), Insect Pest Prevention and Management (IPPM) and Agriculture Water Quality Programs.

ODA has identified datasets related to Food Safety as the most valuable resources to publish for the next biennium (2023-25).  ODA will be entering a planning phase regarding these Food Safety datasets for the remainder of the current biennium and will plan on publishing these datasets in July of 2023.  These datasets will encompass food safety inspection, violation and food recall information.

The Oregon Department of Aviation (ODAV) has a mission to provide infrastructure, financial resources, and expertise to ensure a safe and efficient air transportation system.

The dataset captured in ODAVâ€™s data inventory includes data in the program areas of Aircraft Registration, Operations and the Aviation System Action Program (ASAP).

ODAV has established data sets to publish next biennium (2023-25). ODAV is currently in the planning phase and has plans to publish these data sets in July of 2023.

The Oregon Department of Education (ODE) oversees the education of over 560,000 students in Oregonâ€™s public K-12 education system. This includes than 1,200 public K-12 schools organized into 197 School Districts and 19 Education Service Districts. Over 100 of these schools are public charter schools. These schools and districts employ over 63,000 teachers, administrators, and other school and district staff.  While ODE isnâ€™t in the classroom directly providing services, we foster equity and excellence for every learner through collaboration with educators, partners, and communities.

ODE collects and maintains data on public school students, staff and schools in order to meet state and federal reporting and evaluation requirements for public education.  ODE has completed an inventory of 61 datasets collected through our Consolidated Collections system.  ODE is working on an inventory of other datasets that the agency holds.  Many of these datasets contain information that is protected from disclosure by state and federal privacy laws, and can only be published in aggregate form.

ODE is still developing the list of data sets to publish, but the priority for publication on data.oregon.gov includes institution-level information that is not covered by federal or state privacy laws. Our plan is to have at least one of these datasets published by January 1, 2023, with more published before the end of the biennium.

The mission of the Oregon Department of Forestry is to serve the people of Oregon by protecting, managing, and promoting stewardship of Oregon's forests to enhance environmental, economic, and community sustainability. 

ODF has identified 20 data sets to be published. These include fire protection/prevention information, forest activity tracking, timber sales and associated activity tracking, recreational use permits, land use agreements with private and federal landowners, forest monitoring and enforcement activities. 

ODF will begin cataloging our current externally accessible datasets in the current biennium with the intent to publish the data on data.oregon.gov by the end of the biennium. Once this is complete ODF will catalog the higher priority data sets.

agency_name: Oregon Department of Fish and Wildlife
agency_open_data_plan:
The Oregon Department of Fish and Wildlifeâ€™s (ODFW) mission is â€œto protect and enhance Oregonâ€™s fish and wildlife and their habitats for use and enjoyment by present and future generations.â€ The agency is responsible for the administration and enforcement of wildlife and commercial fishing laws; licenses and permits; hunting, angling, commercial fishing, and trapping regulations; and wildlife protective measures. The agency consists of two main program areas, Administration and Operations. The Administration Program area includes Human Resources, Information Systems, Information and Education, and Administrative Services. The Operations Program has three main divisions (Fish, Wildlife, and Habitat), as well as two regional sectors (East and West). ODFW staff operate out of 24 district and field offices, 35 hatchery facilities, and 22 wildlife areas.

ODFW is currently in the planning phase of the Open Data effort. The dataset inventory for the agencyâ€™s Administration Program area divisions was completed in spring 2022. The agency will complete/finalize the dataset inventory for the Operations Program and regional sectors and plan for future data publication between now and 2024.

The Oregon Department of Energy helps Oregonians make informed decisions and maintain a resilient and affordable energy system. We advance solutions to shape an equitable clean energy transition, protect the environment and public health, and responsibly balance energy needs and impacts for current and future generations.  We have identified three datasets maintained by our agency to be published on data.oregon.gov during the next biennium- our CREP database, Schools database, and Wildfire Recovery Assistance database.
CREP (Community Renewable Energy Grant Program) aims to support the planning and construction of community renewable energy projects in Oregon. The Oregon Department of Energy selected 21 recipients for a total of $12 million in funding in the first round. Notable projects from this first round include: construction of two microgrid systems that pair renewable solar and battery storage to provide energy and resilience benefits to the Tribal buildings of the Confederated Tribes of Coos, Lower Umpqua and Siuslaw Indians; â€œresilience hubsâ€ in the cities of Joseph, Wallowa, and Enterprise â€“ each hub will pair renewable energy generation with battery storage and electric vehicle charging; and the construction of a 107.8-kilowatt solar project with battery storage at a local elementary school in the Jackson County school district. The dataset contains information associated with 68 grant applications to the program after the first round with additional rounds of funding available through 2024. We plan to update this dataset annually.
The Schools Database tracks energy usage data, energy audit measure data, electric vehicles data and electric chargersâ€™ data for Public Purpose Charge Schools Program and other Schools Programs administered by ODOE.  There are 111 eligible School Districts with approximately 825 schools within the Public Purpose Charge Schools Program that report data at least annually but can be multiple times throughout the year.   The energy usage data is monthly data for all eligible schools reported annually and the energy audits and energy measures are entered as they are analyzed by Audit Firms throughout the year.  Final costs for completed energy measures are reported as projects are completed.  The number of energy measures entered and installed each year varies between 50 and 300 measures.  Once published we will likely update this dataset on an annual basis.
The Wildfire Recovery Assistance Database contains information regarding applications for energy efficient rebuilding of residential and commercial structure destroyed by the 2020 wildfires. Over 500 requests have been reviewed for the program with over 2 million dollars requested for recovery, and nearly 1.2 million dollars already paid out to recipients. The database contains data on assistance requests, recipients, and payment status. We will publish new datasets from this program annually.

TRANSPORTATION, DEPT OF The Oregon Department of Transportation exercises leadership and vision in promoting, developing and managing a statewide network of transportation systems and facilities. These systems and facilities provide access to Oregon for the Stateâ€™s citizens and visitors, provides efficient movement of commerce, goods and services, ensures the safety of transportation systems users, and enhances Oregonâ€™s competitive position in national and international markets. ODOT understands its role in stewarding the valuable data assets it collects and manages and enabling the state and its residents to benefit from their use.  

ODOT has undertaken the following activities in support of completing a data inventory and facilitating data publication on the open data portal:     

â€¢ Developed an evolving data taxonomy that allows potential users to easily identify data by subject areas rather than by organizational structure or system (both of which change more frequently than subject areas).     
â€¢ Interviewed business lines regarding their most requested and high value data and data products.     
â€¢ Developed with Ms. Helmsâ€™ assistance a draft inventory sample, as the detailed data inventory ODOT is developing for its own use does not align directly with needs for the enterprise inventory.     
â€¢ Developed an application and user interface for ODOTâ€™s data inventory, which will be used to validate data needed both for the agency and enterprise inventories.     
â€¢ Has begun, in concert with EIS staff, an open data pilot project to aid in development of ODOTâ€™s publishing process.  
â€¢ Hired a dedicated Data Librarian to act as the ODOT Open Data Coordinator.

Through the course of these actions ODOT has determined that, due to the number of distinct program areas, vast data collections, the time needed for inventory validation, and roll-up for agency submission, the agency will submit a single complete (as of that date) data inventory on December 15, 2023, rather than providing quarterly increments. 
In addition to these activities, ODOT over the remainder of the 2022-2024 Open Data Plan period will:     

â€¢ Identify data the agency already publishes and assessing whether to migrate to or provide links in the Open Data Portal     
â€¢ Develop ODOTâ€™s open data publishing and update processes, which includes transferring ownership of the current assets in the Open Data Portal to the Open Data Coordinator
â€¢ Develop and communicate agency open data publishing guidance or a policy

The Oregon Department of Veteransâ€™ Affairs works to serve Oregonâ€™s military veterans, their families, and dependents through a series of services, outreach programs, grants, educational opportunities, and by supporting Oregonâ€™s VA home loan program. The ODVA is responsible for veteran advocacy, state programs, access to federal benefits and policy on behalf of Oregon veterans. Through these wide-ranging responsibilities, the ODVA produces and utilizes a number of different datasets with varying levels of data.

The ODVA is conducting an initial inventory to identify datasets and their prioritization for publishing. At this time initial steps are being taken to identify data owners, coordinate discussions around data curation, and the creation of an approval process for data publishing. Our goal for the next calendar year is to complete this initial identification and curation process so that publication of datasets may begin.

Oregon Employment Department Open Data Plan

The Oregon Employment Department (OED) supports economic stability for Oregonians and communities during times of unemployment through the payment of unemployment benefits; serves businesses by recruiting and referring the best qualified applicants to jobs, and provides resources to diverse job seekers in support of their employment needs; develops and distributes quality workforce and economic information to promote informed decision making; and provides easily accessible paid leave benefits that help Oregon employers and workers maintain quality of life, economic stability, and peace of mind.

The 52 datasets captured within the OED inventory encapsulate the data created as each program area carries out their daily work. The Workforce and Economic Research Division of OED maintains QualityInfo.org, which houses diverse economic and workforce information. Twelve of the highest priority datasets are currently published to QualityInfo.org. The agency plan is to mature the Qualityinfo.org data offerings against the 5 star model for open data, and begin publishing to data.oregon.gov next biennium. Publication will include 1-3 datasets that are accessed most frequently, and include a link to Qualityinfo.org.

Oregon Film & Video Office (OFVO) has aÂ mission to promote, support and advance the film, video, interactive, animation and creative content industries within the state by fostering connections between storytellers, productions, businesses and public sectors to strengthen and diversify this industryâ€™s profile and reputation locally, nationally and internationally. OFVO manages the state media incentive programs: the Oregon Production Investment Fund (OPIF) and the Greenlight Oregon Labor Rebate program (GOLR). Within the OPIF program there are two sub-programs focused on local production (â€œLocalâ€ OPIF or L-OPIF) and regional production work (â€œRegionalâ€ OPIF or R-OPIF). In addition, in conjunction with the Department of Revenue, OFVO manages an annual Tax Credit Auction that auctions off discounted state tax credits every year to fund the OPIF program.

OFVO currently publishes and maintains FOUR datasets. Those are - 1) the OPIF Tax Credit Auction Results, 2) OPIF Reimbursements (which includes L-OPIF Reimbursements and R-OPIF Reimbursements), 3) GOLR Reimbursements and 4) payments made through the Creative Opportunity Program. The first dataset details the purchasers of the tax credits, the amounts paid and the tax credit amounts issued to each purchaser. The remaining four datasets detail the payments made, to whom and for what amount, for each media incentive program as well as the outcomes of those payments. These datasets are updated in the 3rd and 4th quarter of every year.

"The Oregon Medical Board (OMB) protects the health, safety, and wellbeing of Oregon citizens by regulating the practice of medicine in a manner that promotes access to quality care. The OMB licenses Medical Doctors (MD), Doctors of Osteopathic Medicine (DO), Podiatric Physicians (DPM), Physician Assistants (PA), and Acupuncturists (LAc). In addition to its licensing functions, the OMB conducts investigations, imposes disciplinary action, and supports rehabilitation, education, and research to further its legislative mandate to protect the citizens of Oregon. The OMB is also responsible for establishing the scope of practice for Emergency Medical Services (EMS) Providers.

The OMB has completed an initial inventory of datasets summarizing the data created by agency divisions in carrying out core business functions. The OMB has identified data related to licenses issued by the agency as the most valuable for publication. This dataset includes license and practice details such as type, status, issue and expiration dates, limitation, specialty, practice location, and public order status. The OMB will be in a planning phase in the upcoming biennium, focusing on preparing the licenses issued dataset for publication on data.oregon.gov in the subsequent biennium. Planning activities will include assessing privacy protections, cleaning the data, and testing. The OMB will also be evaluating existing and new datasets as part of a business suite system modernization project currently underway."

Agency Mission 

The Oregon Government Ethics Commission (OGEC) will impartially and effectively administer and enforce Oregon's government ethics laws for the benefit of Oregon's citizens.  The Commission will emphasize education in achieving its mission.  

Agency Function 

Comprised of a nine-member citizen commission and nine staff members, OGEC is charged with enforcing Oregon Government Ethics law, Oregon Revised Statute (ORS) Chapter 244, Oregon lobbying regulations, ORS 171.725 to 171.785 and 171.992, and the executive session provisions of Oregon Public Meetings law, ORS 192.660 and 192.685. ORS Chapter 244 relates to financial conflicts of interest, prohibited use of position or office for personal financial gain, nepotism, and gifts to public officials. Additionally, ORS Chapter 244 requires the filing of annual statements of economic interest by certain public officials and candidates. The lobbying regulations in ORS 171.725 to 171.785 require lobbyists and their employers or clients to register with OGEC and file quarterly reports of their lobbying expenditures. Finally, ORS 192.660 relates to the criteria a governing body must use when convening an executive session. OGEC enforces these laws through a complaint and investigation process. In addition to enforcement, OGEC provides verbal and written advice to public officials, issues advisory opinions, and provides training for public officials and lobbyists.  

Datasets and Open Data Phase 

OGEC is currently in the publishing open data phase as the agency plans to publish data within this current biennium (2021-2023). OGEC plans to publish the following multiple datasets: lobbyist registrations, lobbyist expenditures, client-employer registrations, client-employer expenditures, SEI Filers, complaints-correspondence, cases, advice, trainings, assessed penalties, filing penalties, and lobbyist required training.  The lobbyist and client-employer datasets will focus on the data collected by lobbyists and their clients when they register to lobby in Oregon, their expenditure reports (money spent for the purpose of lobbying), and required trainings. The complaints-correspondence and cases datasets will report on the data, such as jurisdiction, issue, location, etc., that is obtained on the complaints filed with OGEC. The advice and trainings datasets will report on the types of advice requested, jurisdictions, and the trainings requested and completed. The penalties datasets will report on the types of penalties assessed by OGEC, the amounts, the reasons for penalties, the jurisdictions, and the collection rate. OGEC plans to publish the datasets before the end of the current biennium.

The Oregon Health Authority (OHA) is the organization at the forefront of lowering and containing costs, improving quality, and increasing access to health care to improve the lifelong health of Oregonians. OHA is responsible for most state health care programs, including Public Health and the Oregon Health Plan. This gives the state greater purchasing and market power to begin tackling issues with costs, quality, lack of preventive care and health care access. Without an accurate and comprehensive view of all agency data and information assets, OHA cannot effectively take data-driven actionable steps to effect statewide change for the health, independence, and care of Oregonâ€™s citizens. 

OHA has set the strategic goal of eliminating health inequities by 2030 and is guided by the following Health Equity definition:

Oregon will have established a health system that creates health equity when all people can reach their full health potential and well-being and are not disadvantaged by their race, ethnicity, language, disability, age, gender, gender identity, sexual orientation, social class, intersections among these communities or identities, or other socially determined circumstances.

Together, the Oregon Health Authority (OHA) and the Oregon Department of Human Services (ODHS) provide critical services to Oregonians services including health care, food assistance, programs for people with disabilities and many others. Due to the complex and interrelated nature our work, OHA and ODHS are partnering to provide one joint inventory of agency datasets and are coordinating on efforts to inventory, model, and map data while planning for enterprise data management and data governance. 

OHA and ODHS are in the PLANNING phase of the Open Data project. We do not anticipate publishing any datasets during the current biennium (beyond those that are already published) due to the preparation and resourcing required to identify and prepare data for publication. The agency is only now beginning its inventory, mapping, modeling, management, and governance planning. Due to the quantity of data systems, number of distinct program areas, and number of data collections, much work remains to identify all datasets and answer Open Data project questions related to each.

Oregon Housing and Community Services provides resources to increase access to stable housing. Our goal is to ensure people have access to homeownership opportunities, provide funds to build and preserve affordable housing, and help stabilize individuals experiencing homelessness by offering services and housing support. We primarily offer funding streams to partners rather than direct services to clients. Due to the nature of our business, we often collect data from our partners for reporting and accountability purposes. Additionally, we offer some direct services like rental assistance and loan products where we are the sole proprietor of those data. The datasets identified in OHCS' data inventory highlight the data created by partners and programs we need to operate on a day-to-day basis.

OHCS is almost complete with its data inventory and should be sending that over to the CDO soon. We plan on reviewing the inventory with management at the agency to determine which datasets we would be able to share in the open data project and it what format as most of our data contain PII and will need to be aggregated or anonymized. OHCS has identified several datasets that can be shared without additional work to redact information. One such dataset we hope to pilot in the current biennium is the Oregon Affordable Housing Inventory. This dataset contains information such as location details, affordability expiration dates by funding source, unit details such as population served, income level served, and number of units with rental assistance, and other housing portfolio data. Additionally, OHCS's Research team is working on creating a data lake to house all data across the agency and to connect data across programs to better understand client outcomes. Our Homeless Services Section is also working on a data lake to house data from their client management system.  This data lake will also connect to the Research team data lake.  We plan of piloting Microsoft Azure within the next year and hope to have the data lake operational in 1-2 years.

Our Mission
OLCC's mission is to support businesses, public safety, and community livability through education and the enforcement of liquor and marijuana laws.

In support of that mission, we issue licenses to companies and organizations that sell liquor and cannabis products and issue permits to the individuals that work for those companies and organizations. As part of the permitting process, we certify server education providers. We also sell the liquor to as a means of limiting access to minors. The monies we collect from the sales and licensing are allocated to the general fund amongst the cities and counties of Oregon for mental health alcoholism & drug services and other revenue sharing.

Open Data Phase
Since we are early in the biennium, I plan to move from Planning to Publishing by the end of the biennium.
I plan to have Liquor Sales data and Revenue Distribution data published. For both data sets, I can modify existing queries, but will need to plan the granularity of the data, draft metadata and a data dictionary, publish the dataset on data.oregon.gov,  and automate dataset updates/publication.

I will begin planning for the license and permit data publication when our new systems start coming online next year.

OSP Open Data Plan (2023-25)

Comprised of over 80 public safety programs, the Oregon State Police (OSP) is a multi-dimensional organization charged with protecting the people, wildlife, and natural resources in Oregon.  To accomplish this charge, OSP enforces the traffic laws on the stateâ€™s roadways, investigates and solves crime, conducts post-mortem examinations and forensic analysis, and provides background checks and law enforcement data.  We regulate gaming and the handling of hazardous materials, and enforce fish, wildlife and natural resource laws.  These programs operate across the state and are interconnected with local, state, regional, and federal organizations.  The datasets identified in OSPâ€™s inventory relate to the activities undertaken by the divisions and programs of the Department in support of its public safety mission.  

As part of the Departmentâ€™s efforts to comply with the State of Oregonâ€™s open data mandate (ORS 276A.350-374) and make its data as findable, accessible, interoperable, and reusable (FAIR) to the public as possible, OSP completed an initial inventory of its core programs and systems.  For open data purposes, OSP considered the datasets with a â€œHighâ€ priority level as they represent the Departmentâ€™s data with the highest level of readiness, value, and publishability.  An evaluation of OSPâ€™s high priority datasets identified several that are already published in a machine-readable format available to the public.  These datasets include Oregonâ€™s Uniform Crime Reporting Offense Data, Hate Crime Data, Arrest Data, and Police Employee Data.  Outside of these datasets, OSP had several other high priority datasets that are shared publicly via a dashboard or report (e.g., citations and warnings, dispatch events, etc.).  Under normal circumstances, OSP would identify these datasets as strong candidates to be published via the data.oregon.gov portal.  Unfortunately, with several of OSPâ€™s major systems undergoing largescale upgrades, the Department does not have the capacity to commit to publishing these (or any other) datasets via the data.oregon.gov portal this biennium (2023-25).  However, OSP will be developing a plan to mature its high priority datasets toward reaching a 5-star open data maturity rating in future biennia and will reevaluate its capacity after these upgrades are completed.

Board Mission:  To protect the public by supervising occupational therapy practice; and to assure safe and ethical delivery of occupational therapy services.

Board responsibilities:

Develops policy and sets standards of practice
Reviews applications and issues licenses to qualified applicants
Investigates complaints and takes appropriate disciplinary action when violations are found to have occurred
Monitors and enforces Oregon Revised Statutes and Administrative Rules pertaining to the practice of occupational therapy in the State of Oregon

The Board is committed to maintaining inventory of information assets and managing data sets.  The Board will ensure both the Executive Director and Administrative Specialist will engage in best practices; remain knowledgeable about Board data, its origins, and is available to answer questions about Board data and/or route questions to the appropriate parties.  The Executive Director and Administrative Specialist will ensure transparency of information that is not protected from disclosure, by making appropriate data available for public consumption, in a manner that will not jeopardize the privacy or security of agency employees, clients or partners.

The Oregon Watershed Enhancement Board (OWEB) is a state agency that provides grants to help Oregonians take care of local streams, rivers, wetlands, and natural areas. OWEB grant-making is led by an 18-member board that makes informed decisions on the allocation of grant funds to support local watershed restoration efforts. Grants are awarded to applicants after a technical review process. OWEBâ€™s staff (44 budgeted positions in OWEBâ€™s 23-25 budget) administer the grant funds.  

OWEB grant recipients include hundreds of local partner organizations, including watershed councils, tribes, local governments, land trusts, schools and universities, and non-profit organizations.  These grant recipients work within their communities to accomplish watershed restoration and habitat improvements on the ground.  They are external users of many of OWEBâ€™s data management systems.  

OWEB maintains core datasets including the Online Grant Management System (OGMS) with information on funding and financial data; Application Review and Management (ARM) with information on grant offerings, applications and evaluations; the Oregon Watershed Restoration Inventory (OWRI) with information submitted by restoration practitioners; the Pacific Coastal Salmon Recovery Fund (PCSRF) database to comply with federal reporting requirements; and several geospatial datasets. 

In the 2021-23 biennium, OWEB used an IT Prioritization process to stage priority tasks and track progress across divisions. OWEBâ€™s Open Data Inventory was completed in March 2022. All Level 1 and Level 2 OWEB datasets are currently published, with redaction where appropriate. In the 2023-25 biennium, OWEB plans to continue work on training and communication about the agencyâ€™s Open Datasets. OWEB anticipates identifying training needs for staff as well as for external customers, and working through cross-section teams to determine how to meet these training needs.

"The Oregon Water Resources Department (WRD) addresses Oregonâ€™s water supply needs by practicing and promoting responsible water management. WRD provides decisions on water right-related applications and transactions, distributes water in the field in accordance with the water rights of record, collects and analyzes groundwater and surface water resources data, conducts well construction and dam safety inspections, and helps individuals, businesses, and communities identify solutions to their water supply needs. The datasets captured within the WRD inventory represent the data created as each division and program area carries out their daily activities. 

WRD has inventoried about 89 total datasets as of June 1, 2022, with 38 datasets prioritized for publication. WRD has identified data related to the dam inventory and critical groundwater area allocations as the most ready and valuable resources to publish this biennium. Publication will likely be preceded by some minor structural improvements and evaluation of compliance with retention schedules. Publication of these datasets will begin after July 2022 and should encompass 2-3 datasets on data.oregon.gov by June 2024. WRD will continue to re-evaluate and make updates to the dataset inventory during this time."

As part of Oregonâ€™s juvenile justice system, The Oregon Youth Authority holds youth accountable and provides them with opportunities for reformation, either on parole or probation in the community, or inside secure facilities.  OYA is responsible for youth ages 12 to 24 who commit crimes before age 18. OYA serves youth who are unsuccessful at the county level, who need more services than the county can provide, or who commit very serious crimes.

County Juvenile Departments and the Oregon Youth Authority share a centralized information system to record information for youth involved in the juvenile justice system (The Juvenile Justice Information System-JJIS).  Because of the nature of the Juvenile Justice system, most records must be de-identified and protected from public release.  However, OYA intends to work with our County Partners to develop data sources that can be made more readily available to the public, while also protecting youth and family confidentiality.  We are currently exploring two potential data sources: National Court Archive Data and improved Annual Report availability for further analysis.

National Court Archive Data:  Annually, juvenile court processing information is used in developing national estimates of juvenile court activities for publication, which is funded by the Office of Juvenile Justice and Delinquency Prevention.  The National Juvenile Court Data Archive (Archive) houses the automated records of cases handled by courts with juvenile jurisdiction. The Archive was established by the Office of Juvenile Justice and Delinquency Prevention, within the U.S. Department of Justice, to promote access to automated juvenile court data sets for juvenile justice research and policymaking efforts. During the 2022-2024 biennium, OYA intends to explore whether this dataset could be made available to the public, or if portions could be provided after youth records have been de-identified.

Annual Reports: OYA currently produces a number of annual reports that summarize data for youth and cases involved in various stages of Oregonâ€™s Juvenile Justice system (Oregon Youth Authority : JJIS Annual Reports : JJIS : State of Oregon).  These reports cover a variety of topics including referrals to the juvenile justice system, secure detention placements, court dispositions, disparities in case processing, etc.  The intent of these reports is to aid in the overall planning, development, and evaluation of programs designed to reduce juvenile crime.  During the 2022-2024 biennium, OYA will explore with our county partners whether de-identified versions of these reports can be made available to the public in alternative formats.  Currently, these reports are only available as a PDF document, which limits interpretation and trend analyses.

The Psychiatric Security Review Board (PSRB) protects the public by working with partnering agencies to ensure persons under its jurisdiction receive the necessary services and support to reduce the risk of future dangerous behavior using recognized principles of risk assessment, victimsâ€™ interest, and person-centered care. The PSRB has jurisdiction over the following individuals:

-	Adults who have been found by the court to be â€œguilty except for insanityâ€ (GEI).
-	Adults who have been found by the court to be an â€œextremely dangerous person with mental illnessâ€.
-	Juveniles who have been found by the court to be â€œresponsible except for insanityâ€ (REI). 

The PSRB oversees the restoration of firearm privileges for individuals who lost their firearm privileges due to mental health determination in the state of Oregon and petition the Board for relief. The PSRB also oversees the Sex Offender Reclassification/Relief program, which allows those with sex offenses in their history to petition the Board to move to a lower classification registration level or be relieved of their obligation to report if currently at the lowest level.

The datasets captured within the PSRBâ€™s Data Inventory contain detailed information regarding the persons under the jurisdiction of the Board contained within the PSRBâ€™s four Microsoft Access databases.

The PSRB is in the PLANNING phase of the Open Data project and anticipates posting their statistical data on data.oregon.gov during the next biennium.

The Oregon Public Utility Commission (PUC) is responsible for rate regulation of Oregon's investor-owned electric utilities (Portland General Electric, Pacific Power, and Idaho Power), natural gas utilities (Avista, Cascade Natural, and NW Natural), telephone service providers (landline only), as well as select water companies. The PUC also enforces electric and natural gas safety standards and handles utility-related dispute resolution on behalf of Oregon residents. In the event of an emergency, the PUC is part of the Oregon Emergency Response System to coordinate and manage state resources.  

Our mission is to ensure Oregon utility customers have access to safe, reliable, and fairly priced utility services that advance state policy and promote the public interest. We evaluate differing viewpoints and visions of the public interest to arrive at balanced, independent decisions that are supported by fact and law. The datasets held by the PUC relate to different aspects of utility operations and evaluation of utility operations, including financial, technical, and safety related information. 

Currently, the PUC will shortly commence implementation of this project. Implementation has been delayed due to a critical software update project that is ongoing. Most PUC datasets are already publicly available on our website. PUC division leads will coordinate cataloging all existing datasets in and inventory process. 

According to our updated open data plan we will do the following: 

The Open Data Coordinator will convene a representative group from across the agency that deal with discrete data categories that the public will have interest in, including: 

The utility program â€“ specifically including representatives of our regulatory program, rates program, and safety program â€“ all of which have unique data sets of interest to the public. 

The consumers division â€“ which deals directly with the public 

The business office â€“ which has important administrative functions and may have some information of public interest and; 

The administrative hearings division â€“ which manages all data related to Commission decisions and case records. 

The Open Data Coordinator will educate the team on the project, working with them to identify key data sets applicable to the project, make judgements as to what should be included as part of the project, and then work with them to build the inventory. 

After the inventory is complete, we plan to meet with DAS for review and discussion of next steps. 

We plan to complete the inventory of data sets: November 22nd, 2023

"The State Library of Oregon is an independent state agency that provides leadership and resources to continue growing vibrant library services for three distinct groups: Oregonians who are print-disabled, the Legislature and state government, and all Oregonians through their local libraries.

We have identified two datasets maintained by our Library Support and Development Services division to be published on data.oregon.gov during the next biennium. The first is an online directory of libraries in Oregon, which contains basic contact information for over 340 entities. The information therein is critical to both the work of this division as well as to the public. Once published we will likely update this dataset on a monthly basis. The second dataset is the Oregon Public Library Statistics, a large annual census of public library data. We will use the data collected during the FY2020-21 period as a starting point and will publish new datasets from this program annually."

The mission of the Board of Licensed Social Workers is to protect the citizens of Oregon through licensing and regulation of Social Workers.

The Bureau of Labor and Industries (BOLI) protects employment rights, advances employment opportunities, and protects access to housing and public accommodations free from discrimination for all Oregonians. It also regulates and supports apprenticeship programs to develop Oregon workforce.

The agency has five main areas that collect and maintain data: Commissioner Office, Civil Rights, Wage and Hour, Apprenticeship, and Employer Assistance. The agency operates out of three physical office locations and has remote employees providing services throughout the state.

BOLI is currently in the planning phase of the Open Data effort and has completed it's partial Data Inventory. As the agency embarks on a database replacement project over the next two years, opportunities for open data will be identified.

Mission: 
The Oregon Board of Pharmacy serves to promote and protect public health, safety, and welfare by ensuring high standards in the practice of pharmacy and through effective regulation of the manufacture and distribution of drugs.

Vision: All Oregonians have equitable access to medication and pharmacy services, provided safely and conveniently, through a network of highly skilled and dedicated Pharmacists, Interns and Pharmacy Technicians along with a well-regulated manufacturing and distribution network.

Equity Statement: The Oregon Board of Pharmacy is committed to Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Belonging (DEIB) within its organization and for the public it serves. This commitment is reflected in board membership, agency staffing, the services provided, and its efforts to promote patient safety and ensure access to quality pharmacy care. Our actions, outlined in our DEIB and Affirmative Action Plans, demonstrate this commitment. 
The following principles guide our approach: 
•	Promote a welcoming, safe, and inclusive culture for people of all backgrounds
•	Foster an inclusive environment where all current and prospective licensees and registrants receive fair and unbiased service from the agency staff and board
•	Advance Diversity and Equity in access through culturally responsive service delivery that addresses the changing climate within the pharmacy profession
•	Ensure all patients needing pharmacy services are able to receive safe and timely access to medications, regardless of place of residence, economic or social status, physical ability, ethnicity, or gender identity

Values: 
These values reflect both how our Board and staff strive to conduct ourselves, and the behaviors we seek to instill across the practice of pharmacy in Oregon.

Equity -  Each individual and group are valued, respected, and treated fairly ensuring equal access to medications and support for their unique and diverse requirements.
Service - We deliver a consistent standard of excellence in all work and respond promptly to the needs of patients, Licensees, Registrants, providers and partners.        
Safety - We are committed to protecting the health, safety and welfare of the public. Safety is the foundation of the board's Mission.
Adaptability - We are open to new ideas and to responding to the changing needs and challenges in the field of healthcare and pharmacy.
Integrity & Accountability - Transparency and honesty govern the board's work. We accept responsibility for our actions, products, decisions, and policies. 
Professionalism - We are committed to promoting excellence in pharmacy practice through expertise, commitment, and competence.

The agency identified data system inventories related to licensing, compliance and communication and submitted a data system inventory in 2023. The Oregon Board of Pharmacy continues to support the Open Data Program, however the agency has not been able to dedicate time or resources to prioritize moving to the next phase. Several factors have contributed to the agency’s ability to dedicate resources to the program such as Executive Director turnover, staff turnover, a record number of compliance cases, a record amount of administrative rulemaking, migrating to Windows 11, current licensing database replacement project, digital accessibility compliance for new federal law, EIS server migration and multiple EIS firewall projects, Board priorities such as immediate access to current COVID-19 and Influenza vaccines for Oregonians, multiple legislative mandates from the 2025 Legislative Session and agency compliance with new Governors Initiatives (DEI plan, IT strategic plan, Succession plan, etc.). The agency currently employees 21 FTE and has 3 open positions and is focused on meeting current service levels.
