The mission of Construction Contractors Board (CCB) is to protect and Serve Oregon Consumers, support responsible licensed contractors and promote a positive business climate.  We do that by delivering customer-focused, outcome-based programs that support a fair and competitive construction industry and robust consumer protections. The datasets captured within the CCB inventory each support one or more of the functions necessary to accomplish our mission.

CCB will continue publication of the “License Information – Currently Active” dataset during upcoming biennium.  Because CCB is currently working on replacing the core business software for all our functions, work on this effort will begin after the start of the fiscal year and will encompass that single dataset on data.oregon.gov.  Work has begun on this effort and we are currently in Stage Gate 2.

The Oregon Criminal Justice Commission (CJC) works to improve the legitimacy, efficiency, and effectiveness of state and local criminal justice systems. The purpose of the Commission is to improve the effectiveness and efficiency of state and local criminal justice systems by providing a centralized and impartial forum for statewide policy development and planning. 

The Oregon Statistical Analysis Center (SAC) is housed at CJC and is the state clearinghouse for criminal justice data and research. As the state Statistical Analysis Center, the agency provides the Governor, Legislature, and public safety partners with access to data, objective research, and analysis related to public safety. While CJC has access to many criminal justice data sources, the agency primarily collects a more limited number of data sources. 

CJC has inventoried six datasets and plans to focus publication on the STOP (Statistical Transparency of Policing) data and Sanctuary Promise reporting data as the agency’s most valuable data sources. CJC will work to publish this data on data.oregon.gov by the end of the current biennium.

agency_name: Department of Consumer and Business Services
agency_open_data_plan:
The Oregon Department of Consumer and Business Services (DCBS) works to protect and serve Oregon’s consumers and workers while supporting a positive business climate. As Oregon’s largest consumer protection and business regulatory agency, DCBS is a resource to consumers and businesses in areas involving finance (insurance, investments, Oregon-chartered banks and credit unions, consumer finance companies, mortgage lenders, pawnbrokers, and payday lenders), workplace health and safety, and building safety. 

DCBS has reviewed and updated the dataset inventory of 96 datasets. We will be in the planning phase during this biennium, with a strong focus on fully identifying existing reports on our agency website and current DCBS datasets being published on the data.oregon.gov portal. Our goals during this reporting period will be to analyze these existing data resources and modify them to ensure compliance with the technical standards set by the Open Data Program. 

DCBS has a complex data infrastructure containing numerous data systems and supporting many distinct program areas. We do not anticipate publishing any new datasets during this biennium. DCBS is in the early stages of data governance planning to guide agency-wide data management efforts. This progression will provide a critical framework for future Open Data efforts and directly support our movement towards prioritization leading to automated publishing on data.oregon.gov.

agency_name: Department of Early Learning and Care
active_agency_name: EARLY LEARNING AND CARE, DEPT OF
agency_open_data_plan:
The Department of Early Learning and Care (DELC) was created by the Oregon Legislature in 2021 to unify and strengthen Oregon’s early learning system. On July 1, 2023, DELC was established, bringing together the Oregon Early Learning Division (from the Department of Education) and the Employment Related Day Care (ERDC) program (from the Department of Human Services). 

As a new Oregon state agency, DELC supports the development and well-being of all Oregon children and ensures families across the state have access to high-quality early learning and care. DELC also supports child care professionals by providing technical assistance, professional development opportunities, business services, licensing, grants, and other resources. 

The mission of the DELC is to foster coordinated, culturally appropriate and family-centered services that recognize and respect the needs of all children, families, and early learning and care professionals. Our vision is that children, families, early care and education professionals, and communities are supported and empowered to thrive. 

To meet these goals, DELC collects data through child care licensing, publicly funded programs, and other related services. This includes information about child care providers and grantees as well as limited information about children and families who receive early education and care services. These datasets help support day-to-day operations and regular reporting requirements. As a new state agency, DELC is in the process of standing up data operations and is in the planning phase for Open Data. Specifically:  

- The agency is engaged in efforts to inventory relevant datasets.  

- Staff anticipate having an inventory of publicly funded programs datasets in 2024. 

- The agency will continue to add information to the inventory with the goal to provide a complete inventory by the end of the biennium.  

In the next biennium, DELC plans to prioritize publishing relevant state-funded programs information to the Open Data Portal. This will help shine light on how those programs are meeting statewide needs. Today, some of that information is publicly available and can be viewed on data dashboards under the agency data landing page of the DELC website. Future submissions to the Open Data Portal will be guided, in part, by state and federal privacy laws that protect some types of data from disclosure or require modifications, such as aggregation, prior to publication.

The Department of Environmental Quality (DEQ)’s mission is to be a leader in restoring, maintaining and enhancing the quality of Oregon's air, land and water. From monitoring and assessing the quality of our air, water and soil, through regulation that reduces pollution, and looking ahead to what climate change may bring, DEQ supports businesses, communities, and municipalities statewide to preserve and protect Oregon's natural resources and human health.

The datasets captured within the DEQ inventory encapsulate the data created, received, or maintained as each program area carries out their daily work. DEQ completed its first inventory in December 2022 with 251 datasets, though we have since identified additional datasets to be added in the next update. We also piloted publication of agency public records requests data to the state open data portal in 2023. 

DEQ will remain in the planning stage this biennium as we complete inventory updates, mature our data governance program, and establish a data stewardship framework. We requested and received an extension on submitting inventory updates until October 2024 pending onboarding of additional staff.

Oregon Department of Human Services (ODHS) serves 1.5 million people each year. ODHS has a significant reach through the ONE Eligibility system, local offices in every corner of the state, subcontracted provider relationships, and mandated responsibilities to protect and serve children, older adults, and people with disabilities. ODHS is guided by our Equity North Star and our commitment to becoming an anti-racist organization. Our strategic direction is shaped by our Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Belonging (DEIB) plan, Strategic Technology plan, RiSE culture initiative, and trauma-informed practices. 

ODHS is in the PLANNING phase for participating in Oregon’s Open Data Program; the agency is engaged in planning and preparation to publish datasets in the future. ODHS submitted an exception request for our data inventory to allow time for the Interim Chief Data Officer to get up to speed and assess the current state of ODHS’ inventory efforts. Further, because we are a large organization with a number of sensitive data sets, it is critical that we take steps to establish data governance and appropriately engage community partners before publishing data. Completing the inventory and taking steps toward creating data governance are the interim CDO's primary goals for the remainder of the calendar year. 

ODHS is aligned with the aims of the Oregon Open Data Program to increase transparency and accountability where data are concerned. We appreciate your patience as we manage this transition and prepare to meet the requirements.

agency_name: Department of Land Conservation and Development
agency_open_data_plan: The Department of Land Conservation and Development (DLCD) is in a planning phase for the Open Data Project. The majority of DLCD datasets are already publicly available via our Oregon.gov website or other available sources. We now have the authority from the Legislature to increase our staff capacity by adding a new data management work group.  In this new model, DLCD will be able to devote the necessary time to develop the plan for preparing the other datasets to be published. We anticipate this task to be taken on by the new agency data management personnel, led by the new CDIO position.  These positions are projected to be filled in the fall of 2024.

The Oregon Department of Corrections (ODOC) is responsible for the safe, secure, and humane management of adults in custody, the supervision of individuals on community supervision, and the provision of constitutionally required services within correctional environments. ODOC operates complex statewide systems that support custody operations, community corrections, health and behavioral health services, investigations, facility security, and statutory reporting obligations.
ODOC has completed a high-level data inventory aligned with statewide data governance guidance and has intentionally prioritized a governance first, risk managed approach to open data planning for this biennium. Due to the sensitive nature of correctional data and extensive statutory, regulatory, and privacy restrictions on disclosure, the majority of ODOC datasets are not appropriate for public release in raw or record level form without formal statutory interpretation, legal review, and data privacy assessment. For the current biennium, ODOC does not anticipate publishing new datasets to data.oregon.gov beyond what is already publicly available through existing statutory reports and dashboards, as no material changes in systems, governance structure, or staffing capacity have occurred that would support expansion of public data publication during this cycle.
ODOC’s ability to expand open data publication in future biennia is directly dependent on progress in foundational areas, including establishment of a formal enterprise data governance and stewardship structure, dedicated data management and metadata capacity, standardized data definitions and quality controls, clear internal approval pathways for public data release, and modernized data infrastructure that supports secure aggregation and disclosure controls. Based on its current inventory and governance capacity, ODOC has identified a limited set of highly aggregated, delayed, and de identified summary datasets derived from existing statutory and public reporting products as the most feasible candidates for future release, such as systemwide population trends, historical admissions and releases summarized by year and facility, high level demographic summaries, and aggregate program participation totals. Any dataset considered for publication would undergo legal review, security review, data quality validation, and executive approval prior to release.

agency_name: Department of Geology And Mineral Industries
agency_open_data_plan:
DOGAMI helps increase the understanding of Oregon geologic resources and hazards through science and stewardship. There are two (2) programs within the agency. (1) The Geological Survey & Services (GS&S) program develops maps, reports, and data to help Oregon manage natural resources and prepare for natural hazards with focus areas including geologic hazards (earthquakes, landslides, floods, and coastal erosion), geologic mapping, and high-resolution lidar data collection. (2) The Mineral Land Regulation & Reclamation (MLRR) program is a field-orientated regulatory program working with industry and the public that oversees the state mineral production and to minimize the impacts of natural resource extraction and maximize opportunities for land reclamation.

In previous years, DOGAMI has undertaken the activities to compile a data inventory and provide it to the Open Data portal. We have identified the locations of DOGAMI publication data inventories.

DOGAMI has previously submitted a listing of items for publishing on data.oregon.gov. This published dataset contains 4,454 records from the GS&S program that publishes geoscience research papers, bulletins, fact sheets, maps, and other documents to disseminate DOGAMI research findings to the public. The publications are currently housed in a searchable repository on the DOGAMI website that contains publications dated as early as 1937. New publications are added to the dataset frequently, averaging close to 20 publications annually.
Our web site is:    https://www.oregon.gov/dogami/pages/default.aspx

Annually, DOGAMI will review our data sources for new publications that will be added to the open data portal.

The Oregon Department of Justice (DOJ), headed by the Attorney General, serves as the state’s law firm. In addition to providing legal representation to state government, DOJ advocates for and protects all Oregonians, especially the most vulnerable, such as children and seniors. The Oregon DOJ and its ten divisions are tasked with running programs related to child support, charitable activities, district attorney assistance, crime victim compensation, and consumer protection. In all, DOJ and the attorney general have responsibility and authority under more than 350 state laws.

We have identified five datasets for the Open Data Program since our initial inventory. The first is the Attorney General Opinions that are accessible through an online database beginning in 1997 that continues to be updated as opinions are requested by public officials and responded to in writing. The second is the Charities Portal which provides registration information and reports from charities registered in Oregon. The Charities Application offers the same dataset in a downloadable zip .txt file that is updated monthly. DOJ’s fourth dataset provides reports of data exposures through the Data Breach public database. The fifth dataset is a catalog of Public Records Exemptions to assist public officials in determining what information is exempt from public disclosure. We will continue maintaining these datasets for the next biennium.

Open Data Plan for June 2024 through June 2026
The Oregon Department of Revenue administers Oregon tax laws, supports partners’ programs, and acts as a central debt collection agency for other state agencies, boards, commissions, and local governments. DOR provide services for the general public, tax professionals, cities, counties, local taxing districts, tribal governments, and other state agencies. The datasets captured within the DOR inventory have to do with tax returns, operations, and financial information for 63 revenue streams managed by the Department. The nature of tax data includes highly confidential information, which cannot be made public in its original form.
DOR has inventoried 193 datasets, approximately 40 of which are already published in some form by DOR or another agency. Last cycle DOR piloted posting some existing datasets to the data.oregon.gov website. In the 24-26 open data cycle DOR intends to expand on the pilot program and publish additional data from existing datasets that are already at a high level of readiness for publication. These datasets will come from the reports and data published by the DOR Research Section.

agency_name: Department of Public Safety Standards and Training

agency_open_data_plan:
Here is our updated Data Plan.  

Upon statehood, the Federal government granted Oregon 3.4 million acres of school lands to support public education. About 775,000 acres of school lands remain in state ownership today, the revenue from which helps fund K-12 public education. The Oregon Department of State Lands manages school lands and oversees the state’s publicly-owned waterways on behalf of the State Land Board. Over time, the Legislature has expanded the Department’s responsibilities to also include protections of regulated waters and wetlands and oversight of Oregon’s national estuarine research reserve. The Department’s mission is to ensure Oregon’s school land legacy and protect waterways and wetlands of the State through superior stewardship and service. The datasets we capture allow us to fulfill that mission.  

DSL has inventoried 19 datasets, most of which are geographic information system (GIS) data that are already published to our own public-facing GIS Portal in the form of maps and data services. Of the remaining datasets, the Land Administration System (LAS) dataset has been identified as having the most value to publish. The permits we issue often have information that is of interest to local communities and Oregonians need to be able to find information about such projects easily. This system, which was created in 1999, is being replaced next year by the Oregon Waters and Land System(OWLS)/State Land Online.  While we currently have two datasets from LAS published on data.oregon.gov, the underlying data structure will change and expand greatly with OWLS, and it will likely replace LAS as the most requested dataset.  When we go live, the LAS dataset will be migrated to OWLS and will be re-published to data.oregon.gov, depending on the need and as our data evolves.

The Employment Relations Board (ERB) provides dispute resolution and administers the collective bargaining law that covers public employees of the State of Oregon and its cities, counties, school districts, and other local governments; hears and decides appeals from state employees concerning personnel actions; and administers the collective bargaining law that regulates private employers who are not covered by the National Labor Relations Act.
The ERB is comprised of the Board, the Hearings Office, and the State Conciliation Service. The three-member Board is a neutral quasi-judicial body whose members are appointed by the Governor and confirmed by the Senate. The Governor selects one member as Board Chair to serve as the agency administrator. Board members apply the law in a neutral and objective manner without regard to their background. 
The Hearings Office is the point-of-entry for contested case and representation filings.  This division conducts hearings on contested cases and issues recommended orders. 
The State Conciliation Service mediates labor relations disputes and provides labor-management and bargaining training to employers and employees. The State Conciliator is the head of the State Conciliation Service.   
The ERB data inventory includes the 10 datasets that will be published in the 23-25 biennium.  ERB will annually publish the 10 existing datasets on data.oregon.gov.  ERB completes its agency data inventory in July of each fiscal year. ERB will continue to evaluate current published data to ensure this data meets the standards for open datasets set by the Open Data Program and revise or expand these datasets as necessary.

The Higher Education Coordinating Commission (HECC) is the State of Oregon’s Commission and agency working to improve equitable access to and success in higher education and workforce training for Oregonians statewide. The HECC administers about $1.8 billion  per year in postsecondary education and workforce funding supporting community colleges, public universities, students, workforce boards, and other partners. We also coordinate postsecondary degrees, programs, and academic pathways; administer key Oregon financial aid, workforce, and other programs; evaluate the success of higher education efforts to guide progress; and provide strategic guidance to leaders and partners on a vision for Oregon higher education planning, funding, and policy. We directly serve Oregonians by awarding grant and scholarship aid to students, and connecting Oregonians with workforce and training resources.

HECC has inventoried nineteen datasets and identified seven that can be published to the Open Data portal. These datasets are related to workforce training programs, community colleges courses, and public universities. These datasets focus on: 
1.)	Dislocated worker events
2.)	Workforce training programs receiving funding from the federal Workforce Innovation Opportunity Act (WIOA)
3.)	Courses of study offered by Oregon community colleges
4.)	Financial overview reports of the Oregon community colleges
5.)	Detailed financial reports of the Oregon community colleges
6.)	Financial overview reports of the Oregon public universities
7.)	Statewide programs offered by Oregon public universities

Publication of these datasets began in July of 2022 and are now in an operational state. HECC will continue to maintain these and evaluate all new data collections to determine if they can be published.

The Office of the Long-Term Care Ombudsman (OLTCO) is an agency that functions separately and independently from any other state agency. The mission of the agency is to protect individual rights, promote independence, and ensure quality of life for Oregonians living in long-term care and residential facilities and for Oregonians with decisional limitations. 

The datasets identified in OLTCO’s inventory embody the data created by each program as they carry out their functions and duties. OLTCO has completed an initial inventory and identified data within the RTZ Get Care dataset as the most valuable to publish this biennium. This dataset contains the complaint investigation and facility visitation from the Long-Term Care Ombudsman program.

Since the agency already reports federally (federal fiscal year – Oct 1-Sept 30) on much of this information, the plan is to keep the same timeframes as is required for the agency’s federal reporting. 

The plan is to report complaint information: complaint codes (types of complaints received), status of those complaints – closed and pending, facility type, city, county, status of complaint, complaint verification, referrals made to other entities, complainant type.

We will report data as of October 2023 for this plan in June 2025.

The Mental Health Regulatory Agency (MHRA) protects the health, safety, and wellbeing of Oregon citizens by overseeing and supporting the boards of Licensed Professional Counselors and Therapists (BLPCT) and Psychology (BOP).  MHRA serves as the operational entity for fulfilling the Boards’ functions of licensing and compliance.  Professions regulated by MHRA include licensed professional counselors (LPC), licensed marriage and family therapists (LMFT), psychologists, and psychologist associates.  

MHRA will accomplish an inventory of datasets during the current biennium.  The agency will focus on data created in carrying out core business functions, to include data related to licenses issued by the agency.  This dataset includes license and practice details such as type, status, issue and expiration dates, practice location, and public order status. 

From the identified inventory, MHRA will identify data for publishing.   This work is expected to begin in the current biennium and continue into future biennia as needed.  As much of this data is already available to the public via the Agency’s licensee lookup function, data will be reviewed and considered for publishing in an aggregated manner.   Activities will include assessing privacy protections, cleaning the data, building an extraction process, testing, and drafting metadata and a data dictionary. MHRA will also be evaluating existing and new datasets as part of a licensing system modernization project currently underway.

The Oregon Advocacy Commissions (OACs), leaders of underrepresented communities in Oregon, share their expertise and knowledge to inform equity in state policy. The OACs research issues, educate the public, and provide advice to state policy and decision makers, including the Governor, legislators, and state agency leadership. The OACs are continually looking for ways to improve the lives and livelihoods of all Oregonians, and encourage diverse leadership in state government.

The Oregon Advocacy Commissions Office (OACO) was established to support the statutory work of all four OACs:

Oregon Commission on Asian & Pacific Islander Affairs (OCAPIA)
Oregon Commission on Black Affairs (OCBA)
Oregon Commission on Hispanic Affairs (OCHA)
Oregon Commission for Women (OCFW)

Currently, the Oregon Advocacy Commissions Office is in the planning phase. We are assessing what dataset we can publish within the next biennium.

The Oregon Board of Dentistry (OBD) is charged with the regulation of the practice of dentistry, dental therapy and dental hygiene by setting standards for entry to practice, examination of applicants, issuance and renewal of licenses, and enforcing the standards of practice. The Board also is required by law to establish standards for the administration of anesthesia in dental offices. The Board determines dental procedures that may be delegated to dental assistants and establishes standards for training and certification of dental assistants. The mission of the Oregon Board of Dentistry is to promote quality oral healthcare and protect all communities in the State of Oregon by equitably and ethically regulating dental professionals.
The OBD has completed an initial inventory and identified data related to licensing and enforcement as the most ready and valued dataset to publish this biennium. This dataset covers OBD’ enforcement activities and licensing data. Publication of this dataset will begin at the beginning of the fiscal year and should encompass 2-4 datasets on data.oregon.gov.

The Oregon Business Development Department, dba Business Oregon, is the state’s economic development agency. Business Oregon invests in Oregon’s businesses, communities, and people to promote a globally competitive, diverse, and inclusive economy, in service of a vision of prosperity for all Oregonians. Core functions include rural community development and infrastructure financing; business retention, expansion, and recruitment; export promotion and international trade; industry research and development and entrepreneurship; small business assistance; and support for arts and cultural organizations. The overall work of the department is guided by a nine-member commission with additional input and expertise provided by several other advisory bodies and boards. Business Oregon’s datasets include the data created as each agency program conducts its activities and daily work. The most recent inventory has datasets that have been combined and refined as well as existing datasets and new datasets resulting from newer programs. 

Business Oregon’s Open Data Plan going forward consists of the following components: further refining inventory where needed, publishing 3-5 additional datasets, and starting to automate the publishing of our agency’s datasets. Business Oregon will continue publishing the 30 existing datasets on data.oregon.gov at an annual frequency and focus on ensuring that metadata and data dictionaries are updated and/or completed for all published datasets.  Business Oregon will continue to further identify and prioritize additional datasets for publication on data.oregon.gov during the ’25-‘27 biennium. Business Oregon will also evaluate current published datasets to ensure these datasets meet any new policies or standards for open datasets set by the Open Data Program and revise or expand these datasets as necessary. Business Oregon commits to publishing 3-5 additional datasets more than last biennium and working towards automating said datasets beyond the current 30 on data.oregon.gov by the beginning of the 2026 fiscal year.

OBMI has inventoried 11 datasets related Applicant information, complaints, and professional training programs as the most valuable resources to publish this biennium.  Publication of these datasets will begin at the beginning of the fiscal year.

Mission Statement: The mission of the Oregon Board of Naturopathic Medicine (Board) is to regulate, through the Oregon Revised Statutes and Administrative Rules, the practices of naturopathic medicine to ensure protection of the public. The Board promotes excellence in the practice of naturopathic medicine by assuring quality education, regulating naturopathic practices, and fostering ongoing public outreach. In this manner, the Board cultivates public trust and safety by ensuring the State of Oregon is served by naturopathic professionals providing the highest standards of care.

Board Responsibilities: Develops policy and sets standards of practice; Reviews applications and issues licenses to qualified applicants; Investigates complaints and takes appropriate disciplinary action when violations are found to have occurred; Monitors and enforces Oregon Revised Statutes and Administrative Rules pertaining to the practice of naturopathic medicine in the State of Oregon.

Board Commitment: The Board is committed to maintaining inventory of information assets and managing data sets.  The Board will ensure both the Executive Director and Administrative Specialist will engage in best practices; remain knowledgeable about Board data, its origins, and is available to answer questions about Board data and/or route questions to the appropriate parties. The Executive Director and Administrative Specialist will ensure transparency of information that is not protected from disclosure, by making appropriate data available for public consumption, in a manner that will not jeopardize the privacy or security of agency employees, clients or partners.

The Oregon Board of Optometry (OBO) is a semi-independent state agency that is responsible for the licensing and regulation of the optometry profession in Oregon. 

The board was established and operates under the authority of ORS 683.010 to 683.340 and 676.110 to 676.230. OAR Chapter 852. OBO is a state agency with a strong commitment to serving the public, consumers, and licensees of the State of Oregon. The primary mission of OBO is consumer protection. This is achieved by promoting the highest standards for licensure through four (4) major program areas: Education, Examinations, Licensing, and Enforcement.

The OBO protects the public by issuing licenses to those individuals who have demonstrated professional competence and continue to maintain their professional competence through continued education. 

OBO is committed to regulating optometry in the State of Oregon without unnecessary regulatory oversight of licensees and by providing fair and accessible entry into the profession that meets the standards and requirements. OBO is a 2-person agency and is able to complete a license application within 1-2 days of receiving all required documentation. Board staff process between 40-50 new license applications each year. 

The Board also processes complaints against licensees. Between 30-40 complaints may be filed each year. 

OBO has inventoried ten (10) datasets and identified datasets related to licensed optometrists licensure status as the most ready and valued dataset to publicsh this biennium. This datasets are the lists of active and inactive status licenses. This dataset includes mailing address information. This dataset is already published on the OBO’s website, and is available for ordering through the website. 

We have reviewed our inventory for this cycle and have published what is currently available. We have additional information available on our website, but it is not available in downloadable format—e.g. our licensee search function that shows licensee history including any disciplinary and malpractice history and copies of the pdf documents.

The Oregon Board of Physical Therapy (OBPT) licenses and regulates physical therapists (PT) and physical therapists assistants (PTA) throughout the state. In the interest of public protection, the Board assures that PTs and PTAs are properly educated, hold current and valid licenses, practice within their scope, and continue to receive ongoing training throughout their careers. 

OBPT has inventoried 5 datasets, of which 3 are already publicly available through the Board’s public-facing licensing portal. These datasets are searchable, but the Board identified in 2022 that publishing an existing dataset on disciplinary actions in a different format for ease of use was a high priority. That dataset is now available on the board's website, as of early 2023. The board intends to expand dataset offerings following the migration of the existing database to an updated platform that allows additional reporting and export options. That work is anticipated to be completed by Fall 2024.

The Oregon Board of Tax Practitioners is dedicated to protecting Oregon Tax Clients by ensuring the highest standards for tax preparation for anyone who wants to complete an Oregon Personal Income Tax Return for a fee. Our mission is to protect Oregon consumers by ensuring Oregon Tax Practitioners are competent and ethical in their professional activities.  The Oregon Board of Tax Practitioners is steadfast commitment to the best interests of our licensees, their clients, and the economy. OBTP has identified data sets that are related to our exams, licensing, renewals and meetings. Allowable publications are published on the OBTP website.

During the current biennium, ODA will continue to maintain existing datasets on data.oregon.gov, on behalf of its Fertilizer Program including product registration, stop sale, use and removal orders, sample results and tonnage.  ODA will also continue to maintain previously published GIS/Map boundary and layer information on ArcGIS Online, regarding its Confined Animal Feeding Operation (CAFO), Insect Pest Prevention and Management (IPPM) and Agriculture Water Quality Programs.
ODA has identified datasets related to Food Safety as the most valuable resources to publish for this biennium (2025-27). ODA is still in the planning phase regarding these Food Safety datasets which encompass food safety inspection, violation and food recall information.

The Oregon Department of Aviation (ODAV) enables and advocates for a safe and efficient aviation system that connects communities and drives economic growth. ODAV's vision is to be a leader in connecting and protecting communities through a resilient, sustainable and technologically advanced aviation system that prepares Oregon for the future. 

ODAV has identified 11 datasets in ODAV’s data inventory which include data in the program areas of Aircraft Registration, Operations and the Aviation System Action Program (ASAP).  ODAV has identified the dataset related to all public use airports in Oregon as the most ready and valuable resource to publish during the current biennium.  This dataset includes airport name, address, type of use, dimensions, surface type, magnetic bearings of runway, elevation and a site map. ODAV has plans to publish this one dataset before July of 2027 to data.oregon.gov.

The Oregon Department of Education (ODE) oversees the education of over 540,000 students in the Oregon public K-12 education system. This includes more than 1,200 public K-12 schools organized into 197 School Districts and 19 Education Service Districts. Over 100 of these schools are public charter schools. These schools and districts employ over 89,000 teachers, administrators, and other school and district staff. While ODE is not in the classroom directly providing services, we foster equity and excellence for every learner through collaboration with educators, partners, and communities.

ODE collects and maintains data on public school students, staff and schools in order to meet state and federal reporting and evaluation requirements for public education.  ODE has completed an inventory of 61 datasets collected through our Consolidated Collections system.  ODE is working on an inventory of other datasets that the agency holds.  Many of these datasets contain information that is protected from disclosure by state and federal privacy laws, and can only be published in aggregate and suppressed format.

ODE is still developing the list of data sets to publish, but the priority for publication on data.oregon.gov includes institution-level information that is not covered by federal or state privacy laws. Our plan is to have at least one of these datasets published by January 1, 2026, with more published before the end of the biennium.

The mission of the Oregon Department of Forestry is to serve the people of Oregon by protecting, managing, and promoting stewardship of Oregon's forests to enhance environmental, economic, and community sustainability.

ODF has identified 19 data sets to be published. These include fire protection/prevention information, forest activity tracking, timber sales and associated activity tracking, recreational use permits, land use agreements with private and federal landowners, forest monitoring and enforcement activities.

ODF will begin cataloging our current externally accessible datasets in the current biennium with the intent to publish the data on data.oregon.gov by the end of the biennium. Once this is complete ODF will catalog the higher priority data sets.

agency_name: Oregon Department of Fish and Wildlife
agency_open_data_plan: The agency convened an Open Data Workgroup in late 2024 that evaluated an existing “Natural Resources” data inventory and submitted an expanded (however still partial) Open Data Inventory to the Open Data Portal in March 2025.  In April 2025 ODFW identified a permanent Open Data Coordinator.  We intend to reconvene the agency Open Data Workgroup in the summer of 2025 to further evaluate and more completely report on previously inventoried datasets by March of 2026.

ODOT 2024-2026 Open Data Plan
TRANSPORTATION, DEPT OF The Oregon Department of Transportation exercises leadership and vision in promoting, developing, and managing a statewide network of transportation systems and facilities. These systems and facilities provide access to Oregon for the State’s citizens and visitors, provides efficient movement of commerce, goods and services, ensures the safety of transportation systems users, and enhances Oregon’s competitive position in national and international markets. ODOT understands its role in stewarding the valuable data assets it collects and manages and enabling the state and its residents to benefit from their use.  

ODOT has undertaken the following activities in support of progressing with the development of ODOT’s data inventory and facilitating data publication on the open data portal:
•	Developed an evolving data taxonomy that allows potential users to easily identify data by subject areas rather than by organizational structure or system (both of which change more frequently than subject areas).     
•	Developed with CDO's assistance a draft summary inventory sample for submission to the Enterprise Data Inventory, as the detailed data inventory ODOT is developing for its own use does not align directly with needs for the enterprise inventory.
•	Hired a dedicated Data Librarian to act as the ODOT Open Data Coordinator.
•	Spent eight months working with ODOT business lines to gather initial list of formal Data Stewards, including Data Trustees (subject matter experts), and another three months getting updates. We are continuing with this identification process.
•	Developed a tool with user interface and training materials for ODOT’s Coordinating Data Stewards (CDS) to accurately track and maintain the list of formal Data Stewards in their designated division, branch, or business area.
•	Designed and began building the structure of a tool with user interface, that will be released in April of 2025, for agency Data Stewards to use to develop and maintain the ODOT Data Inventory (ODIN).

Through the course of these actions ODOT has determined that, due to the length of time it has taken to identify Data Trustees (subject matter experts), time needed to train them about the inventory and on the new tool, and time for them to update and add to the data inventory, the agency has determined it is best to move to a minimum of quarterly submissions to the enterprise inventory beginning no later than April 1, 2025, with continuing submissions every three months.

In addition to these activities, ODOT 2024-2026 Open Data Plan period will:     
•	Identify data the agency already publishes and assess whether to migrate to or provide links in the Open Data Portal     
•	Develop ODOT’s open data publishing and update processes, which includes transferring ownership of the current assets in the Open Data Portal to the Open Data Coordinator
•	Develop and communicate agency open data publishing guidance or a policy.

The Oregon Department of Veterans Affairs works to serve Oregon's military veterans, their families, and dependents through a series of services, outreach programs, grants, educational opportunities, and by supporting Oregon's VA home loan program. The ODVA is responsible for veteran advocacy, state programs, access to federal benefits and policy on behalf of Oregon veterans. Through these wide-ranging responsibilities, the ODVA produces and utilizes a number of different datasets with varying levels of data.

The ODVA is conducting an initial inventory to identify datasets and their prioritization for publishing. Currently ODVA is taking the initial steps to identify data owners, and creation of an approval process for data publishing. Our goal for the next calendar year is to complete this initial identification and approval process so that publication of datasets may begin.

Oregon Employment Department Open Data Plan
August 30, 2024

The Oregon Employment Department (OED) supports economic stability for Oregonians and communities during times of unemployment through the payment of unemployment benefits; serves businesses by recruiting and referring the best qualified applicants to jobs, and provides resources to diverse job seekers in support of their employment needs; develops and distributes quality workforce and economic information to promote informed decision making; and provides easily accessible paid leave benefits that help Oregon employers and workers maintain quality of life, economic stability, and peace of mind.

The datasets captured within the OED inventory encapsulate the data created as each program area carries out their daily work. The Workforce and Economic Research Division of OED maintains QualityInfo.org, which houses diverse economic and workforce information. Twelve of the highest priority datasets are currently published to QualityInfo.org. The agency plan is to mature the Qualityinfo.org data offerings against the 5-star model for open data and begin publishing to data.oregon.gov in the 2023 – 2025 biennium. Publication will include 1-3 datasets that are accessed most frequently; and include a link to Qualityinfo.org.

It is the mission of the Oregon Department of Emergency Management to lead collaborative state-wide efforts, inclusive of all partners and the communities we serve, to ensure capability to get help in an emergency and to protect, mitigate, prepare for, respond to, and recover from emergencies or disasters regardless of cause.?

The OEM Information Technology team has built a project portfolio focused on modernizing core systems, improving interoperability, and increasing the efficiency and impact of emergency management technology across the state. Projects are prioritized based on alignment with agency strategic goals, scalability, and ability to improve outcomes for the communities we serve.

This open data plan focuses on the basic requirements necessary to meet the agency's objectives in alignment with the State of Oregon's Open Data Initiative. The agency recently assigned an agency open data coordinator and lead data steward to staff this function.

For this next cycle, initiation of a data inventory will be accomplished via the newly appointed agency data steward/open data coordinator. This work will be facilitated by this individual working with each section within the agency. We will determine the scope and breadth of the data maintained within the agency – with a specific focus on structured data in databases, SharePoint lists, and others. This will be an agency-wide initiative and will take considerable staff time to get the “lay of the land” for what is currently done and where that information is stored. Success will be measured by how many sections have contributed content, who has been delegated as individual data stewards within each section, and the completion of the inventory list. Take note that this action is currently scoped for initiating this activity – only because we do not know the full amount of data that the agency currently maintains.

It is our goal to determine specific datasets for sharing within the Open Data platform based on already-existing data served out to the public. As the inventory is accomplished, additional datasets will be shared as applicable.

Overall, it is the agency's prerogative to share necessary datasets to keep our partners and the public informed of necessary data in the emergency management sector, allowing and facilitating a more informed and prepared state.

Oregon Film & Video Office (OFVO) has a mission to promote, support and advance the film, video, interactive, animation and creative content industries within the state by fostering connections between storytellers, productions, businesses and public sectors to strengthen and diversify this industry’s profile and reputation locally, nationally and internationally. OFVO manages the state media incentive programs: the Oregon Production Investment Fund (OPIF) and the Greenlight Oregon Labor Rebate program (GOLR). Within the OPIF program there are two sub-programs focused on local production (“Local” OPIF or L-OPIF) and regional production work (“Regional” OPIF or R-OPIF). In addition, in conjunction with the Department of Revenue, OFVO manages an annual Tax Credit Auction that auctions off discounted state tax credits every year to fund the OPIF program.

OFVO currently publishes and maintains FOUR datasets. Those are - 1) the OPIF Tax Credit Auction Results, 2) OPIF Reimbursements (which includes L-OPIF Reimbursements and R-OPIF Reimbursements), 3) GOLR Reimbursements and 4) payments made through the Creative Opportunity Program. The first dataset details the purchasers of the tax credits, the amounts paid and the tax credit amounts issued to each purchaser. The remaining four datasets detail the payments made, to whom and for what amount, for each media incentive program as well as the outcomes of those payments. These datasets are updated in the 3rd and 4th quarter of every year.

Agency Mission

The Oregon Government Ethics Commission (OGEC) will impartially and effectively administer and enforce Oregon's Government Ethics Laws for the benefit of Oregon's citizens.  The Commission will emphasize education in achieving its mission.  

OGEC's jurisdiction is Government Ethics, Lobby and Public Meetings Laws.

Agency Function

Comprised of a nine-member citizen commission and fifteen staff members, OGEC is charged with enforcing Oregon Government Ethics Law, Oregon Revised Statute (ORS) Chapter 244, Oregon Lobby Law, ORS 171.725 to 171.785 and 171.992, and Oregon Public Meetings Law, ORS 192.610 and 192.705. The 2023 Legislative session passed House Bill 2805 which expanded OGEC’s jurisdiction from the executive session provisions of Public Meetings Law to its entirety. ORS Chapter 244 relates to financial conflicts of interest, prohibited use of position or office for personal financial gain, nepotism, and gifts to public officials. Additionally, ORS Chapter 244 requires the filing of annual statements of economic interest by certain public officials and candidates. The lobby laws in ORS 171.725 to 171.785 require lobbyists and their employers or clients to register with OGEC and file quarterly reports of their lobbying expenditures. Finally, Public Meetings Law is based upon the policy that the Oregon form of government requires an informed public aware of the deliberations and decisions of governing bodies and the information upon which such decisions were made. It is the intent of ORS 192.610 to 192.705 that decisions of governing bodies be arrived at openly. OGEC enforces these laws through a complaint and investigation process. Public Meetings Law includes a grievance process which is designed to allow governing bodies to cure alleged violations. In addition to enforcement, OGEC provides verbal and written advice to public officials, issues advisory opinions, and provides training for public officials and lobbyists.  

Datasets and Open Data Phase

OGEC is currently in the planning stage open data phase as the agency is currently adjusting to its newest jurisdiction and the additional datasets that will become available with Public Meetings Law. OGEC is currently planning to publish the following multiple datasets: lobbyist registrations, lobbyist expenditures, client-employer registrations, client-employer expenditures, SEI Filers, complaints-correspondence, cases, advice, trainings, assessed penalties, filing penalties, and lobbyist required training.  The lobbyist and client-employer datasets will focus on the data collected by lobbyists and their clients when they register to lobby in Oregon, their expenditure reports (money spent for the purpose of lobbying), and required trainings. The complaints-correspondence and cases datasets will report on the data, such as jurisdiction, issue, location, etc., that is obtained on the complaints filed with OGEC. The advice and trainings datasets will report on the types of advice requested, jurisdictions, and the trainings requested and completed. The penalties datasets will report on the types of penalties assessed by OGEC, the amounts, the reasons for penalties, the jurisdictions, and the collection rate. OGEC is currently analyzing the additional datasets that addition of Public Meetings Law will bring, such as the list of approved Public Meetings Law trainings.

The Oregon Health Authority (OHA) is the organization at the forefront of lowering and containing costs, improving quality, and increasing access to health care to improve the lifelong health of Oregonians. OHA is responsible for most state health care programs, including Public Health and the Oregon Health Plan. The Health Authority is working to fundamentally improve how health care is delivered and paid for, but because poor health is only partially due to lack of medical care, OHA will also be working to reduce health disparities and to broaden the state's focus on prevention.
OHA remains in the PLANNING phase of the Open Data project while efforts continue to establish agency data governance, engage with partners to meaningful plan for data publication, and improve data management technologies. 

In April 2024, an agency reorganization resulted in the establishment of the Office of Data Strategy and Operations within the Agency Operations Division with the purpose to promote, elevate and align equity in OHA service delivery using data and systems. This office will lead the planning, assessment, and determination of strategic direction for enterprise-wide data management and governance.

Due to the size of OHA and the complexity of data it is custodian to, OHA is engaging with industry leaders to support data management and governance initiatives.  In early 2023, the agency partnered with Info-Tech Research Group to conduct a current state assessment of OHA’s data management maturity, to develop a high-level data strategy, and to develop a high-level implementation roadmap of data governance initiatives. This work is being continued through a twelve-month contract with contractor BerryDunn initiated in April 2024. As a result of this current engagement, OHA will establish a strategic Data Governance Council and a tactical Data Governance Steering Committee to begin creating strategic, tactical, and operational layers of data governance necessary for the effective management of data. 

OHA also recognizes its role in both setting the conditions for and supporting action on community-led data justice efforts. As custodian to a vast amount of data, it is imperative that data publication be reflective of community-driven needs, challenges and strengths; representative of community; and that it is managed in ways that promote community self-determination (Coalition of Communities of Color). Simply put, OHA should not alone be determining which datasets are of high public value but rather should empower communities to tell us what they need and how they’d like their data to be used. This effort requires careful planning and extensive community engagement that will span multiple years. Failure to reflect community need and values in our published data would materially jeopardize OHA’s efforts to eliminate health inequities. 
Simultaneously, an engagement is underway to assess opportunities for data cataloging, data quality, and data inventory tools to better describe and continuously manage OHA’s existing data landscape. Following this assessment, OHA anticipates procuring a solution or set of solutions that facilitate more strategic use of OHA-managed data. 

By prioritizing data governance, empowering community to do data justice and advancing data management, we can ensure that the data we publish is not only accurate and reliable but is also representative of those we serve.

Our Mission: OLCC's mission is to support businesses, public safety, and community livability through education and the enforcement of liquor and marijuana laws. In support of that mission, we issue licenses to companies and organizations that sell liquor and cannabis products, and issue permits to the individuals that work for those companies and organizations. As part of the permitting process, we certify server education providers. We also sell the liquor to as a means of limiting access to minors. The monies we collect from the sales and licensing are allocated to the general fund amongst the cities and counties of Oregon for mental health alcoholism & drug services and other revenue sharing.


We are currently publishing liquor pricing data and plan to have Liquor Sales data, Marijuana sales data and Revenue Distribution data published by the end of the biennium. The process of moving to a new licensing, permitting and compliance system is almost complete and we expect to be publishing that data late in this biennium or early in the next.

The Oregon Medical Board (OMB) protects the health, safety, and wellbeing of Oregon citizens by regulating the practice of medicine in a manner that promotes access to quality care. The OMB licenses Medical Doctors (MD), Doctors of Osteopathic Medicine (DO), Podiatric Physicians (DPM), Physician Assistants/Associates (PA), and Acupuncturists (LAc). In addition to its licensing functions, the OMB conducts investigations, imposes disciplinary action, and supports rehabilitation, education, and research to further its legislative mandate to protect the citizens of Oregon. The OMB is also responsible for establishing the scope of practice for Emergency Medical Services (EMS) Providers.

The OMB updated an inventory of datasets summarizing the data created by agency divisions in carrying out core business functions and identified data related to licenses issued by the agency as the most valuable for publication. This dataset includes license and practice details such as type, status, issue and expiration dates, limitation, specialty, practice location, and public order status. The OMB will be in a publishing phase in the upcoming biennium, focusing on preparing the dataset of licenses issued for publication on data.oregon.gov at some point in the latter part of the biennium. Activities will include assessing privacy protections, cleaning the data, building an extraction process, testing, and drafting metadata and a data dictionary. The OMB will also be evaluating existing and new datasets as part of a business suite system modernization project currently underway.

The Oregon Racing Commission is dedicated to promoting the highest standards of safety and integrity within the equine racing industry, both on-ground and online. Our mission is to safeguard the welfare of athletes, both human and equine, with a steadfast commitment to the best interests of our citizenry, licensees, participants, and the economy.
ORC has identified data sets that are related to greyhound and horse racing and data sets that are related to licenses for OTB’s, ADW’s and Fair meets. Allowable publications are published on the ORC website

The Oregon State Board of Nursing (OSBN) protects the public through the regulation of nursing and promoting the wellness of nursing professionals. It regulates certified nursing assistants, certified medication aides, licensed practical nurses, registered nurses, and advanced practice registered nurses by setting educational and training standards, standards for licensure, and compliance with the Nurse Practice Act.

The OSBN has updated an inventory of datasets summarizing the data created by agency divisions in carrying out core business functions and identified data related to licensing, education, and enforcement as the most ready and valued datasets to publish during the upcoming biennium. This dataset includes license and practice details such as license type, status, issue and expiration dates, and public disciplinary order status. The OSBN will be in a planning phase in the upcoming biennium, focusing on preparing the dataset of licenses issued for publication on data.oregon.gov at some point in the latter part of the biennium.

agency_name: Office of the State Fire Marshal
agency_open_data_plan:
The Oregon State Fire Marshal (OSFM) is committed to its mission, to protect people, property, and the environment from fire and hazardous materials. In alignment with this mission, the OSFM's open data plan is designed to enhance the accessibility and utility of fire, hazardous materials, and community safety data for the public, fire service, and other partners. The plan will focus on improving existing high-priority datasets already published on the OSFM’s website (approximately 7 – 9 datasets), ensuring they meet the four or five-star rating on the Open Data Maturity Rating. This includes making datasets available in a downloadable .CSV format, providing an API endpoint for data download, and including comprehensive metadata and data dictionaries. To ensure future consistency with this initiative, an agency-wide procedure will be established to ensure that all new datasets published on the OSFM website adhere to the four or five-star rating.  

Furthermore, in accordance with ORS 276A.350-374, the OSFM will also publish the metadata to the Oregon Open Data Portal as "External Datasets" and link to the dataset(s) on the OSFM’s website. This will facilitate the findability, accessibility, interoperability, and reusability (FAIR) of data, allowing for increased visibility and discoverability by various stakeholders. 

By implementing these measures, the OSFM aims to foster a culture of openness and innovation, enabling better decision-making and enhancing public safety through more informed community engagement and resource allocation. The open data plan not only aligns with the OSFM's mission but also supports the broader goals of transparency and accountability in government operations, ensuring that Oregon remains a leader in data-driven emergency response and fire prevention strategies.

The Oregon State Marine Board (OSMB) is Oregon's recreational boating agency. Our mission is to serve Oregon's recreational boating public through education, enforcement, access, and environmental stewardship for a safe and enjoyable experience. OSMB is in the publishing phase of the Open Data Standard. Our goal is to bring a selection of high-priority datasets to a five-star open data rating. Meeting this goal is realized by updating and refreshing datasets in the Data.Oregon.Gov portal, creating metadata and improving public datasets currently available through public web applications such as GIS layers, or datasets available to download in other formats like .csv. The datasets identified in OSMB's inventory embody the data created by the agency for each section and program area to carry out its mission. Preparation and publication of the datasets will continue through the biennium.

POLICE, OREGON STATE	OSP Open Data Plan (2024-26)  

Comprised of over 80 public safety programs, the Oregon State Police (OSP) is a multi-dimensional organization charged with protecting the people, wildlife, and natural resources in Oregon.  To accomplish this charge, OSP enforces the traffic laws on the state’s roadways, investigates and solves crime, conducts post-mortem examinations and forensic analysis, and provides background checks and law enforcement data.  We regulate gaming and the handling of hazardous materials, and enforce fish, wildlife and natural resource laws.  These programs operate across the state and are interconnected with local, state, regional, and federal organizations.  The datasets identified in OSP’s inventory relate to the activities undertaken by the divisions and programs of the Department in support of its public safety mission.    

As part of the Department’s efforts to comply with the State of Oregon’s open data mandate (ORS 276A.350-374) and make its data as findable, accessible, interoperable, and reusable (FAIR) to the public as possible, OSP completed an initial inventory of its core programs and systems.  For open data purposes, OSP considered the datasets with a “High” priority level as they represent the Department’s data with the highest level of readiness, value, and publishability.  An evaluation of OSP’s high priority datasets identified several that are already published in a machine-readable format available to the public.  These datasets include Oregon’s Uniform Crime Reporting Offense Data, Hate Crime Data, Arrest Data, and Police Employee Data.  Outside of these datasets, OSP had several other high priority datasets that are shared publicly via a dashboard or report (e.g., citations and warnings, dispatch events, etc.).  Under normal circumstances, OSP would identify these datasets as strong candidates to be published via the data.oregon.gov portal.  Unfortunately, with several of OSP’s major systems undergoing largescale upgrades, the Department does not have the capacity to commit to publishing these (or any other) datasets via the data.oregon.gov portal this biennium (2024-26).  However, OSP will be developing a plan to mature its high priority datasets toward reaching a 5-star open data maturity rating in future biennia and will reevaluate its capacity after these upgrades are completed.

The Oregon Watershed Enhancement Board (OWEB) is a state agency that provides grants to help Oregonians take care of local streams, rivers, wetlands, and natural areas. OWEB grant-making is led by an 18-member board that makes informed decisions on the allocation of grant funds to support local watershed restoration efforts. Grants are awarded to applicants after a technical review process. OWEB’s staff (44 budgeted positions in OWEB’s 23-25 budget) administer the grant funds.  

OWEB grant recipients include hundreds of local partner organizations, including watershed councils, tribes, local governments, land trusts, schools and universities, and non-profit organizations.  These grant recipients work within their communities to accomplish watershed restoration and habitat improvements on the ground.  They are external users of many of OWEB’s data management systems.  

OWEB maintains core datasets including the Online Grant Management System (OGMS) with information on funding and financial data; Application Review and Management (ARM) with information on grant offerings, applications and evaluations; the Oregon Watershed Restoration Inventory (OWRI) with information submitted by restoration practitioners; the Pacific Coastal Salmon Recovery Fund (PCSRF) database to comply with federal reporting requirements; and several geospatial datasets. 

OWEB uses an IT Prioritization process to stage priority tasks and track progress across divisions. OWEB’s Open Data Inventory is complete, and all Level 1 and Level 2 OWEB datasets are currently published. OWEB continually works to maintain, update, and provide quality control on all agency datasets; and to provide internal and external trainings.

"The Oregon Water Resources Department (WRD) addresses Oregon’s water supply needs by practicing and promoting responsible water management. WRD provides decisions on water right-related applications and transactions, distributes water in the field in accordance with the water rights of record, collects and analyzes groundwater and surface water resources data, conducts well construction and dam safety inspections, and helps individuals, businesses, and communities identify solutions to their water supply needs. The datasets captured within the WRD inventory represent the data created as each division and program area carries out their daily activities. 

WRD has inventoried about 89 total datasets as of June 1, 2022, with 38 datasets prioritized for publication. WRD has identified data related to the dam inventory and critical groundwater area allocations as the most ready and valuable resources to publish this biennium. Publication will likely be preceded by some minor structural improvements and evaluation of compliance with retention schedules. Publication of 2-3 datasets is expected by June 2025. WRD will continue to re-evaluate and make updates to the dataset inventory during this time."

The Psychiatric Security Review Board (PSRB) was created by the Oregon Legislature in 1977 for the purpose of assuming jurisdiction of those persons who successfully assert the insanity defense to a criminal charge. The Board is multi-disciplinary in nature and was originally comprised by statute of five part-time members (psychiatrist, psychologist, attorney, parole and probation officer, and a member of the public). It bridges the criminal justice and mental health systems. Its enabling statutes direct the Board to have as its primary purpose the protection of society while remaining mindful of the rights of individuals under its jurisdiction. Its mission is to protect the public through the on-going review of the progress of guilty except for insanity clients and a determination of their appropriate placement. It does that through a hearings process in which a panel of Board members meets to review individual cases in order to determine which clients are in need of hospital level of care and which can be adequately supervised and treated in the community under conditions set and monitored by the Board. Approximately 75% of the cases supervised by the Board are for serious person-to-person felony charges such as Assault, Arson, and Homicide.

In 2005, the Oregon Legislature expanded the role and responsibilities of the PSRB. It created a juvenile panel of the Board to assume jurisdiction over youths who were found by a juvenile court to be “responsible except for insanity” of a crime and who suffer from a “serious mental condition” or any mental disease or defect and pose as substantial danger to others. The statute took effect for youth with mental illness on July 1, 2007 and for youth with developmental disabilities on January 1, 2008. The panel is comprised by law of five part- time members including a child psychiatrist, child psychologist, an attorney experienced in juvenile law, a juvenile court counselor and a member of the general public. 

In 2009, the Oregon Legislature yet again expanded the role of the PSRB by assigning it the responsibility of conducting gun relief hearings to determine whether persons with mental health determinations who are barred from possessing a firearm should have their firearm privileges restored. The 2013 Legislative session expanded the Board's role in granting relief by designating it as the authority to hold hearings for those persons who have successfully asserted the guilty except for insanity defense and wish relief from reporting as a sex offender or reclassification of their risk designation. Additionally, the Legislature provided Oregon courts the option of placing certain civilly committed individuals under the Board's jurisdiction for placement and supervision purposes.
 
The PSRB data inventory includes four datasets, one for each major population under its jurisdiction:  GEI clients, REI clients, Gun Relief Petitioners, and Civilly Committed Individuals.  Due to the nature of information collected by the Board to aide in its decision-making process, these datasets contain considerable confidential personally identifiable information protected by HIPPA, the FBI’s CJIS Security Policy, and the provisions included in chapter 192 of the Oregon Revised Statues, and it will take time to sanitize the datasets to ensure that private personal information is not released to the public.  PSRB has identified the civil commit population information as valuable and interesting to the public and will therefore focus on publication of the civilly committed individuals’ dataset during the current biennium.

The Oregon Public Utility Commission (PUC)’s mission is to ensure Oregonians have access to safe, reliable, and fairly priced utility services that advance state policy and promote the public interest. We use an inclusive process to evaluate differing viewpoints and visions of the public interest and arrive at balanced, well-reasoned, independent decisions supported by fact and law.  The PUC is responsible for rate regulation of Oregon's investor-owned electric utilities, natural gas utilities, landline telephone service providers, as well as select water companies. The PUC also enforces electric and natural gas safety standards and handles utility-related dispute resolution on behalf of Oregon residents. In the event of an emergency, the PUC is part of the Oregon Emergency Response System to coordinate and manage state resources. 

Implementation was delayed due to staffing limitations in conjunction with an ongoing critical software update project. The PUC has taken steps to remedy the staffing limitations and allow the software update to run concurrently with the open data program inventory. The PUC plans to have the inventory completed by April 2025, with the goal of being able to publish one to two data sets by June 2026.  The PUC will remain in the planning stage this biennium as we work to complete our initial inventory.

The State Library of Oregon is an independent state agency that provides leadership and resources to continue growing vibrant library services for three distinct groups: Oregonians who are print-disabled, the Legislature and state government employees, and all Oregonians through their local libraries.

The State Library has currently published, and is maintaining, two datasets from our Library Support and Development Services division that were identified in our initial inventory for the Open Data Program: the Oregon Library Directory (updated quarterly) and the Oregon Public Library Statistics (updated annually, with data from FY2010-present).  

Since our initial data inventory process, we have identified two additional datasets that we are currently assessing for the Open Data Program. One set contains a small amount of branch/outlet-level data that is collected annually through the Oregon Public Library Statistics Program. The other is an informal collection of GIS shapefiles and associated metadata pertaining to public library service areas. This collection was gathered informally for internal informational needs, and we are looking to rebuild this collection using updated shapefiles from authoritative sources.
